Velenosi  Ninfa Marche Rosso IGT
short_description: Marche red blend of depth and structure—blackcurrant and cedar with firm tannins for serious red-meat pairing.
description: Velenosi's Ninfa Marche Rosso IGT is a multi-varietal red blend anchored by Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Montepulciano, Sangiovese, and Shiraz. The wine presents a full-bodied profile with dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, supported by pronounced cedar and oak character from winemaking. Medium acidity and medium-high tannins provide structure and aging potential. Secondary notes of black pepper and spice emerge alongside the oak framework, while tertiary complexity—tobacco and leather—develops with bottle age. This is a serious food wine suited to grilled red meats, braised beef, lamb, and aged cheeses.

name: Baron Philippe de Rothschild  Mouton Cadet Bordeaux Rouge
short_description: Elegant Bordeaux blend of Merlot, Cabernet Sauvignon & Franc; ripe dark fruit, balanced structure, food-friendly.
description: A classic Bordeaux red from the prestigious Baron Philippe de Rothschild portfolio, this Mouton Cadet blend showcases the house's commitment to accessible yet refined winemaking. The blend of 60% Merlot, 20% Cabernet Sauvignon, and 20% Cabernet Franc delivers a harmonious expression of dark cherry, plum, and subtle cassis on the nose, with a soft, medium-bodied palate that balances ripe fruit with gentle cedar and herbaceous notes. The wine's moderate tannin structure and natural acidity make it an ideal companion to a wide range of cuisines, from grilled meats and braised dishes to tomato-based preparations and charcuterie.

name: Bread & Butter  Pinot Noir
short_description: California Pinot Noir built on a deliberate MLF-and-oak manifesto — fruit-forward cool-climate sourcing, French and American barrel aging at 13.5% ABV.
description:
When Gregory Ahn launched Bread & Butter in 2013, the brand name was a winemaking position statement: oak = bread, malolactic fermentation = butter — a conscious counter-punch to the lean, unoaked California style that had dominated the preceding decade. The Pinot Noir follows that same philosophy, sourced from cool-climate California sites spanning Pacific-influenced coastal blocks where marine air moderates ripening and preserves aromatic lift in the grape's notoriously thin skin.

Because Bread & Butter contracts all fruit rather than owning vineyards, winemaker Linda Trotta has latitude to blend across multiple cool-climate appellations — drawing on Carneros fog-belt sites and coastal Sonoma blocks — to hit a consistent house profile vintage to vintage. The wine is aged in a combination of French and American oak barrels, the French component contributing fine-grained spice and the American adding the vanilla-inflected roundness that defines the brand's signature texture. ABV is held at 13.5%, moderate for California Pinot.

What separates this from most entry-tier California Pinot Noir is the brand's explicit commitment to the MLF-and-oak axis at scale: a style architecture usually reserved for higher price points, delivered here through multi-regional blending discipline rather than single-vineyard prestige. The current vintage maintains the house's ripe dark cherry and dried rose character, with the oak regime adding a cocoa-dusted finish — approachable on release and built for near-term drinking.

name: Glaetzer  Bishop
short_description: Barossa Valley Shiraz with ripe blackberry, plum, and oak spice—a medium-full red for fine dining and grilled meats.
description: Glaetzer Bishop is a Barossa Valley Shiraz expressing the region's signature dark-fruit character. Blackberry and plum dominate the palate, supported by oak-derived vanilla and black pepper spice. Subtle smoke and cocoa notes add complexity and food-friendly structure. Medium-full body with balanced tannin and acidity positions this wine as a versatile companion to grilled red meat, lamb, and braised dishes.

The wine opens with pronounced floral and honeysuckle aromatics, followed by lychee, peach, and tropical-fruit notes on the palate. A subtle honey sweetness and bright citrus acidity create a refreshing, approachable profile ideal for casual enjoyment or pairing with Asian cuisine and light desserts.

name: Zonin  Lambrusco
short_description: Vibrant Italian sparkling with red berries, crisp acidity, and fine bubbles—perfect for celebrations and Asian cuisine.
description: Zonin's Lambrusco is a lively sparkling wine from Emilia-Romagna, Italy's historic heartland of effervescent reds. Bright red fruit dominates the palate, supported by crisp acidity and delicate bubbles that dance on the tongue. Notes of berries and citrus zest give way to subtle brioche complexity, making this wine equally at home as an aperitif or paired with spicy Asian dishes, dim sum, and light appetizers.

name: Wolf Blass  Eaglehawk Shiraz
short_description: Barossa Valley Shiraz with ripe blackberry, plum, and subtle oak—a food-friendly medium-full red for fine dining.
description: Wolf Blass Eaglehawk Shiraz from Barossa Valley delivers a classic Australian expression of the varietal. Ripe blackberry and plum dominate the palate, supported by vanilla and oak from winemaking, with black pepper spice and a whisper of smoke adding complexity. Medium-full body and balanced tannin make this wine approachable yet structured, ideal for pairing with grilled red meat, braised beef, or roasted poultry. A reliable, well-crafted choice for everyday fine dining and special occasions alike.

name: Wolf Blass  Eaglehawk Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Barossa Valley Cabernet with ripe dark fruit, vanilla oak, and peppery spice—a food-friendly classic.
description: Wolf Blass Eaglehawk Cabernet Sauvignon from Barossa Valley delivers a medium-full-bodied expression of Australia's signature red-wine style. Ripe blackberry and plum dominate the primary aromatics, supported by secondary notes of vanilla and toasted oak from careful winemaking. A subtle tertiary layer of smoke and black pepper adds complexity and structure. The wine's balanced tannin and medium acidity make it an approachable yet serious partner for grilled meats, braised beef, and roasted lamb.

name: Botter  Primitivo Puglia (Organic Wine)
short_description: Organic Primitivo from Puglia: bold, fruit-driven red with smooth tannins and peppery spice. Excellent value for everyday drinking.
description: Botter's Primitivo from Puglia delivers a bold, fruit-forward expression of this southern Italian varietal. Dark cherry and raspberry dominate the palate, supported by subtle smoke and black pepper notes that add complexity without overwhelming. The wine's medium-full body and rounded tannin structure create a smooth, approachable mouthfeel—ideal for casual entertaining and food-friendly occasions. Organic certification reflects sustainable viticulture in the warm Puglian climate, where Primitivo thrives. This is an unpretentious, well-made red that prioritizes drinkability and value.

name: Wolf Blass  Eaglehawk Cuvee Brut
short_description: Elegant Australian sparkling with green apple, citrus, and brioche complexity. Versatile aperitif and seafood companion.
description: Wolf Blass Eaglehawk Cuvée Brut is a refined Australian sparkling wine from the Barossa Valley, crafted from Chardonnay to deliver bright, food-friendly character. The wine opens with primary notes of green apple and citrus zest, followed by secondary winemaking complexity of brioche and toasted oak. A mineral backbone and medium-high acidity provide structure and elegance, making this cuvée equally at home as an aperitif or paired with seafood and delicate cuisine. The non-vintage format ensures consistent quality and approachability across releases.

name: Wolf Blass  Eaglehawk Chardonnay
short_description: Barossa Valley Chardonnay with ripe stone fruit, citrus, and elegant oak—a versatile white for fine dining.
description: Wolf Blass Eaglehawk Chardonnay from Barossa Valley presents a medium-full-bodied white wine with balanced acidity and subtle complexity. The primary character showcases ripe green apple, citrus zest, and stone fruit, complemented by secondary notes of vanilla oak, butter, and toasted hazelnut. A touch of minerality emerges on the finish, adding refinement and food-friendliness. This wine bridges casual enjoyment and fine-dining occasions, pairing well with shellfish, cream-based pasta, roasted poultry, and aged cheeses.

name: Hennessy  V.S (With Box) (700 ml)
short_description: Founded 1765 on the Charente; VS matured in new Limousin oak, distilled on-lees for a richer, congener-dense house signature across four crus.
description: When Maurice Hennessy codified the VS star-rating system, he wasn't simply labeling a product — he was establishing the vocabulary by which the entire cognac industry would grade itself for generations. That institutional confidence is baked into the spirit itself. Ugni Blanc grapes, harvested from over 1,500 independent partner growers across Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, Borderies, and Fins Bois, are fermented with native wild yeast for 5–7 days — no added sugar, no sulphur — producing a deliberately thin, high-acid base wine of 8–10% ABV designed to drive the alembic charentais hard. Double distillation in traditional Charentais copper pot stills concentrates the spirit to ~70% ABV; crucially, Hennessy distills both with and without lees and reintroduces heads and tails into the brouillis on the second pass — a deliberate congener-loading decision that separates its profile from Martell's lees-free, lighter approach. VS is matured in new Limousin oak from Hennessy's own forests (trees felled at 80+ years), coopered by hand in-house at a rate of 400–500 barrels per year. Cellars near the humid Charente riverbank yield rounder eaux-de-vie; drier inland chais contribute structure. The result is a dark-fruited, oak-forward entry-tier expression that carries the full weight of the house's assertive style — unusual at the VS minimum two-year age threshold.

name: Batasiolo  Moscato Spumante Dolce
short_description: Aromatic Moscato Spumante from Piedmont with lychee, peach, and honeyed florals—elegant aperitif sparkle.
description:
Batasiolo's Moscato Spumante Dolce is a lightly sparkling wine from Piedmont's renowned Moscato region, crafted entirely from the Moscato grape. The wine opens with pronounced lychee and peach aromatics, complemented by delicate floral notes and honeyed sweetness. Fine, persistent bubbles deliver a graceful mouthfeel, while bright acidity provides balance to the wine's inherent sweetness. The brioche undertone from the sparkling process adds subtle complexity without overwhelming the fruit-forward character.

This is a quintessential Italian aperitif wine—approachable, aromatic, and versatile at table. Its light body and moderate sweetness make it equally at home as an opening glass or paired with fruit-based desserts and Asian cuisine.

name: Luigi Bosca  Malbec
short_description: Argentine Malbec from Mendoza with ripe blackberry, plum, and cocoa; medium-full body and smooth tannins.
description: Luigi Bosca Malbec is a full-flavored Argentine red from Mendoza, showcasing the signature richness and structure of the region's flagship varietal. Aromas of blackberry and plum dominate the nose, complemented by violet florality and cocoa undertones from French oak aging. On the palate, the wine displays medium-full body with well-integrated, smooth tannins and balanced acidity. Earthy minerality and subtle spice notes add complexity and food-friendliness. This is a versatile, approachable Malbec suited to grilled meats, braised dishes, and casual entertaining.

name: Bread & Butter  Chardonnay
short_description: A winemaking manifesto in a bottle: 100% MLF plus dual-oak aging built to revive California's buttery Chardonnay archetype at scale.
description:
When Gregory Ahn launched Bread & Butter in 2013, the brand name was a deliberate provocation: the California market had over-rotated toward lean, unoaked Chardonnay, and Ahn encoded his counter-argument directly into the label — oak = bread, malolactic fermentation = butter. The brand exceeded 200,000 cases within four years without national chain distribution, validating the thesis entirely.

Fruit is sourced from two climatically opposed California zones: cool Pacific-influenced sites in Sonoma/Carneros, where marine fog suppresses sugar accumulation and preserves natural acidity, blended with Monterey/Arroyo Seco blocks where the Salinas Valley's afternoon wind tunnel produces concentrated, aromatic fruit despite the coastal latitude. No single-site soil profile dominates; the multi-regional blend is the architecture.

The two lots are aged separately before blending: Sonoma fruit spends approximately 14 months in 1-year-old American oak, building vanilla and coconut-tinged spice with softer extraction; Monterey fruit spends approximately 8 months in new French oak, adding finer-grained toast and structure. Both lots undergo 100% malolactic fermentation, converting malic to lactic acid across the entire blend. ABV is 13.5%.

The special feature is the deliberate two-oak, two-region split-aging architecture — a blending strategy that uses American oak's broader grain for texture and French oak's tighter grain for aromatic lift, then unifies them through complete MLF.

Consistent house style across vintages; drink on release through 2–3 years for peak fruit expression.

name: Plantation  Original Dark Rum (700 ml)
short_description: Multi-island molasses blend double-aged tropically then finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks at Château de Bonbonnet, Charentes — reviving 18th-century European dock-ageing.
description: Alexandre Gabriel built Plantation around a single transgression: treating rum the way Cognac treats eau-de-vie. The Original Dark is the house's entry-point expression of that philosophy — a multi-island molasses-based blend that undergoes two distinct maturation stages before bottling. Stage one takes place in the country of origin, in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels under tropical heat, where the angel's share runs at 6–7% per year — more than double the rate in France — concentrating the distillate rapidly. Stage two moves the rum by sea to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, where ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks layer dried-fruit character and baking-spice structure onto the Caribbean base. The molasses feedstock — sourced across multiple islands — delivers the dense, caramelised backbone that defines the blend. What sets Original Dark apart within the Plantation range is its role as the most direct expression of the double-ageing concept in stripped-back, accessible form: no single-vintage complexity, no cask-strength intensity, just the core architecture of the method at 40–42% ABV. Select batches receive the house's Cognac-style dosage — toasted brown sugar aged one year in Ferrand Cognac barrels — openly disclosed where applied, a transparency rare in the category.

name: Glenfarclas  Single Highland Malt Whisky 12 Years (700 ml)
short_description: Direct gas-fired Speyside malt from Ben Rinnes, sixth-generation Grant family, matured exclusively in Spanish Oloroso sherry butts at 43% ABV.
description: When the Grant family reverted from steam coils back to direct gas-fired pot stills within weeks of switching in the early 1980s, it was because the spirit had lost something irreplaceable — the Maillard-driven caramelisation and oily density that only open flame beneath copper produces. That decision defines every drop of Glenfarclas 12 Year Old. Rechlerich Farm, Ballindalloch, Banffshire — at the north-western foot of Ben Rinnes at roughly 244 m elevation — supplies process water from the mountain's own springs: soft, lightly mineral, and consistent across decades. The distillery's three wash stills (~29,600 L each) and three spirit stills (~25,000 L each) run slowly, with pronounced reflux through large boil balls, building a robust new make filled into cask at 63.5% ABV. Maturation proceeds in Spanish Oloroso sherry butts sourced exclusively from José Miguel Martín, held in traditional dunnage warehouses — earth floors, stone walls, three stacks high — where temperature swings are gradual and extraction measured. The 12 Year Old is bottled at 43% ABV with natural colour and no chill-filtration omitted only at 46%+, placing it at the richer, more structured end of age-stated Speyside. Uniquely, Glenfarclas maintains no NAS expressions in its core range — every bottle carries a declared age, a discipline almost no peer sustains commercially.

name: Kilchoman  Machir Bay  (700 ml)
short_description: Islay single malt with robust peat smoke, coastal brine, and warming oak—a full-bodied dram for serious whisky enthusiasts.
description: Kilchoman Machir Bay is a non-age-statement single malt Scotch whisky from Islay, Scotland's most peat-forward whisky region. The spirit delivers a characteristic smoky profile anchored by maritime peat, complemented by heathery floral notes and dark fruit undertones. Maturation in oak casks imparts vanilla sweetness and caramel warmth, while extended aging introduces layers of black pepper spice and subtle dried seaweed minerality. The medium-full body and balanced structure make it approachable yet complex, suitable for neat sipping or with a splash of water to open the palate.

name: Jim Beam  White Label Straight Bourbon Whiskey (700 ml)
short_description: Kentucky straight bourbon with vanilla oak and subtle spice; approachable everyday whiskey.
description: Jim Beam White Label is a Kentucky straight bourbon whiskey offering a straightforward, accessible profile. The spirit leads with vanilla and oak character from barrel maturation, supported by gentle spice notes and a smooth, warming finish. Designed for versatility—sipped neat, on the rocks, or in classic cocktails—this commercial-grade bourbon delivers consistent flavor without complexity.

name: Concha Y Toro  Reservado Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Chilean Cabernet Sauvignon from Maipo Valley with dark fruit, cedar, and balanced structure—food-friendly everyday premium.
description: Concha y Toro's Reservado Cabernet Sauvignon from Chile's Maipo Valley delivers a classic expression of the varietal with medium-full body and approachable tannin structure. Dark cherry and blackcurrant dominate the nose, supported by cedar and subtle vanilla oak from careful aging. The palate shows good balance between fruit intensity and acidity, with layers of black pepper and cocoa that evolve toward herbaceous and earthy notes. A versatile, well-crafted wine suited to everyday entertaining and food pairing.

name: Torbreck  Shiraz Woodcutter's
short_description: Ripe Barossa Shiraz with blackberry, plum, and oak—a food-friendly, approachable red for fine dining.
description: Torbreck's Woodcutter's Shiraz is a medium-full-bodied red from Australia's Barossa Valley, built on a foundation of ripe blackberry and plum fruit. Oak and vanilla from winemaking add structure and warmth, while black pepper and subtle smoke provide complexity. The wine balances richness with accessibility, making it equally at home with grilled meats, braised beef, or roasted poultry. A versatile, well-crafted expression of Shiraz suited to both casual entertaining and fine dining occasions.

name: Martinborough Vineyard  Pinot Noir Te Tera
short_description: Elegant Martinborough Pinot Noir with bright red fruit, silky tannins, and mineral-driven freshness.
description: Martinborough Vineyard's Te Tera Pinot Noir showcases the region's signature elegance and restraint. Bright raspberry and red cherry dominate the palate, supported by subtle earthy minerality and a silky, refined tannin structure. High acidity provides freshness and food-friendliness, making this wine equally at home as an aperitif or paired with lighter proteins and vegetable-forward dishes. A classic expression of New Zealand Pinot Noir terroir.

name: Luccarelli  Negroamaro Puglia IGP
short_description: Smooth Negroamaro from Puglia with dark fruit, smoke, and peppery spice. Full-bodied, food-friendly, and eminently drinkable.
description: Luccarelli's Negroamaro from Puglia delivers a bold, fruit-forward profile anchored by dark cherry and raspberry with supporting notes of smoke and black pepper. Medium-full bodied with rounded, well-integrated tannins, this wine balances richness with approachability. The palate shows good structure and complexity without heaviness, making it an excellent everyday red for grilled meats, tomato-based pasta, and Mediterranean cuisine.

name: Viu Manent  Estate Collection Reserva Merlot
short_description: Colchagua Merlot with ripe dark fruit, cocoa, and approachable tannins—a versatile Chilean red for everyday dining.
description: Viu Manent's Estate Collection Reserva Merlot from Colchagua Valley presents a classic expression of the varietal in its Chilean context. The wine displays ripe plum and black cherry on the nose, supported by subtle cocoa and spice notes from careful oak aging. On the palate, medium-full body and soft, integrated tannins create an approachable yet structured wine suitable for a range of table settings—from grilled meats and braised beef to roasted poultry and tomato-based pasta dishes.

name: Viu Manent  Estate Collection Reserva Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Colchagua Cabernet with dark fruit depth, cedar spice, and food-friendly structure—a reliable Chilean red for grilled meats and aged cheese.
description: Viu Manent's Estate Collection Reserva Cabernet Sauvignon from Colchagua Valley presents a classic Chilean expression of the varietal. Dominated by blackcurrant and dark cherry on the nose, with supporting notes of cedar and cocoa from careful oak aging, the wine develops a medium-full body and balanced tannin structure suited to serious food pairing. A touch of Malbec (3%) adds subtle plum complexity and textural refinement. On the palate, spice and dark fruit lead, with tertiary tobacco and leather emerging as the wine opens, suggesting modest aging potential. Ideal for grilled red meat, braised beef, or lamb dishes; equally at home with aged hard cheeses and earthy preparations.

name: Little Beauty  Marlborough 'Limited Edition' Pinot Noir
short_description: Elegant Marlborough Pinot Noir with bright red fruit, silky tannins, and fresh minerality—a versatile summer wine.
description: Little Beauty's Limited Edition Marlborough Pinot Noir showcases the region's signature elegance and restraint. Bright red fruit and raspberry dominate the nose and palate, supported by subtle earth and mineral notes that speak to the vineyard's terroir. The wine's silky tannin structure and vibrant acidity make it refreshing and food-friendly, equally at home with roasted poultry, mushroom risotto, or charcuterie. A graceful expression of New Zealand Pinot Noir that rewards both casual sipping and thoughtful pairing.

name: Campo Viejo  Winemaker's Art (750 ml)
short_description: Bold Tempranillo from Rioja with ripe plum, cherry, and leather—a versatile, well-structured red for grilled meats and hearty fare.
description: Campo Viejo's Winemaker's Art is a single-varietal Tempranillo from Spain's prestigious Rioja region, crafted to deliver approachable complexity at a competitive price point. The wine opens with dominant dark plum and red cherry aromatics, supported by subtle vanilla oak and leather undertones that reflect careful winemaking. Medium-full body and balanced acidity make it food-friendly across a range of occasions—from casual weeknight dinners to more formal entertaining. The finish is long and dry, with emerging tobacco and dried herb notes that add intrigue to the mid-palate.

name: Valdubon  Roble
short_description: Smooth, fruit-forward Tempranillo from Ribera del Duero with ripe dark berries, balanced tannins, and approachable oak aging.
description: Valdubon Roble is a classically styled Tempranillo from Spain's prestigious Ribiera del Duero region. This single-varietal red showcases the hallmark character of the appellation: deep, ripe dark fruit—blackcurrant and dark plum—supported by medium tannins and a well-integrated oak structure. The palate is smooth and inviting, with subtle spice and vanilla notes from barrel aging, finishing with mineral complexity and a lingering fruit-driven close. A food-friendly expression that bridges approachability with genuine depth.

name: Baron Philippe de Rothschild  Mouton Cadet Heritage Bordeaux Rouge
short_description: Approachable Bordeaux blend of ripe plum and cherry with balanced tannins; food-friendly classic.
description: Mouton Cadet Heritage is a well-balanced Bordeaux red built on a foundation of Merlot (95%) with supporting Cabernet Sauvignon (5%). The wine opens with dominant dark plum and black cherry fruit, complemented by subtle cassis undertones. A restrained oak influence adds gentle spice and vanilla notes without overwhelming the fruit-forward character. Medium tannins and balanced acidity make this wine approachable in youth while retaining enough structure for food pairing versatility. A classic expression of Bordeaux's commercial strength, designed for everyday enjoyment and table compatibility.

name: Voyager Estate  The Modern Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Margaret River Cabernet with ripe blackcurrant, cedar oak, and structured tannins—a modern classic.
description: Voyager Estate's Modern Cabernet Sauvignon from Margaret River showcases the region's signature dark fruit intensity and structural elegance. Ripe blackcurrant and black cherry dominate the primary profile, supported by pronounced cedar and oak from careful winemaking. The palate is full-bodied with high, well-integrated tannins that frame subtle tobacco and earth notes, suggesting complexity and aging potential. A contemporary expression of Australian Cabernet crafted for serious collectors and food pairing enthusiasts.

name: Corryton Burge  Barossa Shiraz
short_description: Full-bodied Barossa Shiraz with dark fruit, spice, and oak—a fine-dining red for grilled meats.
description: Corryton Burge's Barossa Valley Shiraz delivers a full-bodied expression of Australia's signature varietal. Blackberry and dark plum dominate the primary aromatics, supported by secondary oak and chocolate notes that speak to careful winemaking. Black pepper and clove spice emerge on the palate, with a smoky undertone that adds complexity. High tannins and medium-high acidity provide structure and aging potential, making this wine a versatile companion to grilled red meat, lamb, and slow-braised beef dishes.

name: Chandon  Rose
short_description: Elegant Australian sparkling rosé with bright citrus, red fruit, and brioche complexity. Versatile aperitif and seafood companion.
description: Chandon Rosé is a refined sparkling wine from Australia's Yarra Valley, crafted from a blend of Chardonnay and Pinot Noir. The wine displays the hallmark characteristics of quality sparkling production: a pale salmon hue, fine persistent bubbles, and an aromatic profile balancing bright citrus and green apple with delicate red fruit notes. On the palate, brioche and toast complexity from the traditional méthode champenoise process adds depth, while crisp acidity and subtle minerality provide structure and elegance. This is a versatile aperitif suitable for celebrations, seafood courses, and light appetizers.

name: Baron Philippe de Rothschild  Mouton Cadet Bordeaux Blanc
short_description: Crisp Bordeaux white blend of Sauvignon Blanc, Sémillon & Muscadelle—citrus, stone fruit & herbaceous minerality.
description: A classic Bordeaux white from the prestigious Baron Philippe de Rothschild portfolio, this blend marries the aromatic intensity of Sauvignon Blanc (65%) with the textural richness of Sémillon (30%) and the delicate floral lift of Muscadelle (5%). On the nose, bright citrus zest and green apple lead, followed by subtle herbaceous and grassy notes. The palate is crisp and mineral-driven, with stone-fruit character and a clean, refreshing finish. Medium body and vibrant acidity make this an exceptionally versatile food wine and an elegant aperitif.

name: Matua  Sauvignon Blanc
short_description: Vibrant Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc with citrus zest, tropical fruit, and herbaceous minerality. Crisp, food-friendly, and refreshing.
description: Matua's Sauvignon Blanc from Marlborough, New Zealand's premier cool-climate region, delivers a vibrant expression of the varietal's signature character. Dominant citrus zest and tropical fruit notes dominate the nose and palate, supported by herbaceous minerality and crisp green apple acidity. The wine's medium body and bright acidity make it an exceptionally versatile food companion, equally at home with raw oysters, grilled white fish, or spicy Asian cuisine. A classic New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc that balances fruit-forward appeal with mineral precision.

name: Little Beauty  Marlborough 'Limited Edition' Sauvignon Blanc
short_description: Vibrant Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc with citrus zest, tropical notes, and crisp minerality—refreshing and food-friendly.
description: Little Beauty's Limited Edition Sauvignon Blanc from Marlborough showcases the region's signature bright acidity and aromatic intensity. Expect dominant citrus zest and tropical fruit on the nose, with green apple and herbaceous undertones that define the primary character. The palate is clean and mineral-driven, with medium body and refreshing acidity that makes this wine exceptionally versatile at the table. A classic expression of New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc crafted for immediate enjoyment.

name: Greywacke  Pinot Gris
short_description: Crisp Marlborough Pinot Gris with citrus zest, stone fruit, and mineral freshness—elegant and food-friendly.
description: Greywacke's Pinot Gris from Marlborough showcases the region's signature clarity and precision. Bright citrus zest and green apple dominate the nose, supported by ripe pear and subtle minerality. The palate is dry and refreshing, with medium body and vibrant acidity that carries the wine through to a clean, lingering finish. This is a versatile white that balances fruit-forward character with structural elegance, making it equally at home as an aperitif or paired with seafood and lighter cuisine.

name: Greywacke  Wild Sauvignon
short_description: Vibrant Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc with citrus zest, herbaceous depth, and mineral precision.
description: Greywacke's Wild Sauvignon is a classic expression of Marlborough terroir, delivering bright citrus zest and herbaceous complexity on the nose. The palate shows green apple and tropical fruit character balanced by crisp acidity and subtle minerality, with a refreshing finish that lingers with grass and lime notes. A versatile white wine suited to seafood, Asian cuisine, and fresh preparations.

name: Mount Riley  Riesling
short_description: Marlborough Riesling with vibrant citrus, stone fruit, and mineral precision—crisp, food-friendly, and refreshingly dry.
description: Mount Riley's Riesling from Marlborough showcases the region's signature precision and brightness. Vibrant citrus zest and green apple dominate the nose, with supporting stone fruit and delicate floral notes. The palate is crisp and mineral-driven, with a clean, refreshing finish that makes this wine exceptionally versatile at the table. Medium body and medium-high acidity position it as an ideal aperitif or pairing partner for seafood, Asian cuisine, and light salads.

name: Mount Riley  Gewurztraminer
short_description: Aromatic Marlborough Gewürztraminer with lychee, rose, and stone fruit—refreshing and food-friendly.
description: Mount Riley's Gewürztraminer showcases the classic aromatic profile of this distinctive varietal, with prominent lychee and rose blossom on the nose, complemented by peach and citrus zest. Medium body and brisk acidity deliver a refreshing palate that balances the wine's natural sweetness with crisp minerality. The floral and spice-driven character makes this an exceptionally versatile food wine, particularly suited to Asian cuisines, soft cheeses, and elegant aperitif service.

name: Little Beauty  Marlborough 'Black Edition' Sauvignon Blanc
short_description: Vibrant Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc with herbaceous intensity, grapefruit drive, and mineral precision.
description: Little Beauty's Black Edition Sauvignon Blanc from Marlborough showcases the region's signature herbaceous character and bright citrus profile. The wine opens with dominant grapefruit and tropical notes, supported by crisp green apple and mineral undertones. High acidity and light body make this a refreshing aperitif and a natural partner for oysters, grilled fish, and fresh goat cheese.

name: Hennessy  X.O (700 ml)
short_description: Maurice Hennessy invented the XO category in 1870 for family use — now a blend of 100+ eaux-de-vie aged up to 30 years in Limousin oak.
description: Maurice Hennessy created the XO designation in 1870 as a private reserve for family and friends — a category he invented, and which the entire cognac industry subsequently adopted. That origin as a personal standard, not a commercial one, still defines the ambition of the blend. Sourced from over 1,500 independent partner growers across Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, Borderies, and Fins Bois, the eaux-de-vie draw on soils ranging from the shallow clay-limestone chalk of Grande Champagne — which drives the structural depth suited to long aging — to the clay-flint of Borderies, which contributes a rounder, more aromatic register. Over 100 eaux-de-vie aged up to approximately 30 years are assembled by the Fillioux family, sole Master Blenders for eight consecutive generations — an unbroken 200-year lineage unique in global spirits. Distillation in traditional Charentais copper pot stills includes both lees-on and lees-off batches, with heads and tails reintroduced to the brouillis before the second pass — a deliberate congener-concentrating technique that separates Hennessy's assertive house profile from lighter-styled peers. Aging rotates between Limousin oak barrels produced at Hennessy's own cooperage, using staves from forests purchased over 40 years ago. The result is a cognac of dark dried fruit, roasted oak, and building spice — bottled at 40% ABV, lightly filtered, non-vintage.

name: Massenez  Poire Prisonniere ( Prisoner Pear ) (700 ml)
short_description: Alsatian pear brandy with a whole pear suspended in the bottle—aromatic, smooth, and elegant.
description: Massenez Poire Prisonnière is a traditional Alsatian fruit brandy featuring a whole pear suspended within the bottle during production. The spirit captures the delicate aromatics and subtle sweetness of ripe pear fruit, delivering a smooth, full-bodied drinking experience characteristic of premium eau-de-vie. The distilled pear character dominates the palate, supported by gentle floral notes and a clean, refined finish. Ideal as a digestif or in dessert preparations.

name: Remy Martin  1738 Accord Royal Cognac (700 ml)
short_description: Fine Champagne cognac distilled on the lees in charentais copper pot stills, aged in century-old Limousin oak — the house that never blends outside its top two crus.
description: The name references 1738, the year Louis XV granted Rémy Martin a royal exemption to expand his vineyards — a privilege that locked the house's identity to Grande and Petite Champagne long before those crus carried legal weight. That territorial commitment is the technical foundation of 1738 Accord Royal: every eau-de-vie in the blend originates exclusively from the two highest-ranked Cognac crus, a restriction most rivals reserve only for prestige cuvées. The deep chalky-calcareous subsoil of Fine Champagne retains moisture through dry seasons, preserving grape acidity and concentrating aromatic compounds in the Ugni Blanc that forms roughly 97% of the base wine. Rémy Martin's defining production signature is distillation on the lees — the residual yeast sediment is left in the still rather than racked off, a deliberate choice that builds aromatic density most peers cannot replicate. Double distillation runs through traditional alembic charentais copper pot stills, each cycle taking approximately 24 hours. Maturation proceeds in French Limousin oak from trees at least 100 years old, wood seasoned outdoors for 2–3 years; the large open grain drives pronounced vanilla and spice exchange. What distinguishes 1738 within the Rémy range is its positioning between VSOP and XO — a richer oak profile than the VSOP achieved through a higher proportion of older Limousin barrels, without the decade-plus minimum age of the XO tier. Non-vintage by AOC regulation; no drinking window applies to a blended spirit of this type.

name: Hennessy  V.S.O.P  Chinese New Year 2025 Limited Edition (700 ml)
short_description: Hennessy VSOP blends 4 Cognac crus — Grande Champagne chalk to Borderies clay-flint — via lees-on double distillation. 2025 CNY collector bottle.
description:
When Maurice Hennessy codified the VSOP designation in the 19th century, he was not merely labeling an age tier — he was institutionalizing a blending philosophy that the house, founded on the Charente riverbanks in 1765, has never abandoned. That philosophy is now carried by the eighth consecutive generation of the Fillioux family as Master Blenders, an unbroken lineage unique in global spirits.

The eaux-de-vie in this blend draw from four distinct crus: the shallow clay-limestone chalk of Grande Champagne (prized for long-aging potential), the clay-flint soils of Borderies, and contributions from Petite Champagne and Fins Bois — each cru adding a different textural and aromatic register. Cellars positioned along the humid Charente riverbank mellow the spirit; drier inland chais build character.

Hennessy's production signature sets it apart from peers: double distillation in Charentais copper pot stills retains both lees-on and lees-off batches, and heads and tails are reintroduced to the brouillis before the second pass, concentrating congeners for greater depth. Aging proceeds in Limousin oak from Hennessy's own forests, cooperaged in-house at 400–500 barrels per year. The VSOP minimum age is four years, though the blend draws from the house's reserve of up to 470,000 casks. The 2025 Chinese New Year limited-edition bottle marks a collectible release at standard 40% ABV, lightly filtered.

name: Hennessy X.O  Chinese New Year 2025 Limited Edition (700 ml)
short_description: Maurice Hennessy invented the XO category in 1870; this 2025 CNY edition bottles that legacy — 100+ eaux-de-vie aged up to 30 years in Limousin oak.
description: Maurice Hennessy created the XO designation in 1870 as a private gift for family and friends — a category the entire cognac industry would eventually adopt. The 2025 Chinese New Year limited edition bottles that founding act in a collector's release built on the same multi-decade architecture. Sourced from over 1,500 independent partner growers across Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, Borderies, and Fins Bois, the blend draws on soils ranging from shallow clay-limestone chalk — the chalky subsoil of Grande Champagne that drives the long-aging potential — to the clay-flint of Borderies, which contributes a rounder, nuttier register. Eaux-de-vie aged in cellars along the humid Charente riverbank yield mellower, rounder spirit; drier inland chais contribute structure and grip. Double-distilled in Charentais copper pot stills, with both lees-on and lees-off batches, and uniquely, heads and tails are reintroduced into the brouillis before the second pass — concentrating congeners for the house's characteristically dense, dark-fruited depth. The XO blend assembles 100+ eaux-de-vie aged up to approximately 30 years in Limousin oak from Hennessy's own forests, cooperaged on-site at 400–500 barrels per year. The Fillioux family — eight consecutive generations as sole Master Blender — assembled this expression. The 2025 CNY packaging marks a limited annual release; the liquid itself is non-vintage, blended for stylistic consistency.

name: Hennessy  2025 MAF V.S.O.P Limited Edition (700 ml)
short_description: MAF 2025 limited-edition VSOP: Hennessy's lees-on distillation and 8-generation Fillioux blending legacy in a collector's release.
description:
When Maurice Hennessy codified the VSOP designation in the 19th century, he was institutionalising a blending philosophy that the Fillioux family — sole Master Blenders for eight unbroken generations — has refined ever since. That lineage is the living engine behind this 2025 MAF Limited Edition.

Eaux-de-vie are drawn from partner growers across the four premier crus: the shallow clay-limestone chalk of Grande Champagne (prized for slow oxidation and structural longevity), the clay-flint soils of Borderies (contributing a rounder, nuttier register), and contributions from Petite Champagne and Fins Bois. Casks resting in humid riverbank chais along the Charente yield mellower spirit; drier inland cellars add grip and character — both are drawn upon in the VSOP blend.

Production follows Hennessy's house transgression: distillation both with and without lees, and heads and tails reintroduced to the brouillis before the second Charentais pot-still pass, concentrating congeners that most houses discard. Base wine is fermented with native wild yeast, no added sugar or sulphur, at 8–10% ABV. Aging proceeds in Limousin oak from Hennessy's own forests, in barrels hand-cooperaged on-site, to a minimum of four years.

What distinguishes this release is the MAF collector packaging — a numbered limited edition anchoring the standard VSOP liquid in an annual-release format rare for a volume cognac house. Bottled at 40% ABV, lightly filtered, non-vintage.

name: Hennessy  2025 MAF X.O Limited Edition (700 ml)
short_description: Hennessy XO in a 2025 MAF limited livery — 100+ eaux-de-vie aged to 30 yrs, distilled on lees in Charentais copper for maximum congener depth.
description: Maurice Hennessy created the XO category in 1870 as a private reserve for family and friends — a blend so ambitious it had no commercial classification yet. That founding logic still governs the liquid inside: over 100 individual eaux-de-vie, the oldest aged up to approximately 30 years, drawn from Grande Champagne's shallow clay-limestone chalk, the clay-flint soils of Borderies, and Fins Bois, each cru contributing a distinct structural layer. Casks near the humid Charente riverbank yield rounder, mellower spirit; drier inland chais contribute grip and character — both are present here. Distillation in traditional Charentais alembic copper pot stills includes both lees-on and lees-off batches, with heads and tails reintroduced to the brouillis before the second pass, concentrating congeners to a depth Martell's lees-free protocol cannot replicate. Aging proceeds in Limousin oak from Hennessy's own forests — trees felled only after 80+ years of growth, cooperaged by hand on-site at a rate of 400–500 barrels per year. The eighth consecutive generation of the Fillioux family assembled this blend, an unbroken 200-year lineage unique in global spirits. The 2025 MAF edition packages this unchanged liquid in a collector's release, making the bottle a limited object while the XO formula itself remains non-vintage by design. Bottled at 40% ABV, lightly filtered.

The spirit leads with bright apple orchard character balanced by vanilla and caramel oak influence, supported by warm spice and subtle toasted grain notes. At 100 proof, the spirit delivers full body and warming alcohol presence, making it equally suited to neat sipping, dessert pairing, or classic cocktail service.

description: When Maurice Hennessy codified the VSOP designation in the 19th century, he was not simply labeling age — he was institutionalizing a blending philosophy that the Fillioux family has executed across eight consecutive generations, an unbroken 200-year partnership without parallel in global spirits. That continuity is the technical backbone of this expression. Sourced from Grande Champagne, Petite Champagne, Borderies, and Fins Bois — soils ranging from shallow clay-limestone chalk in Grande Champagne (the cru prized for long-aging potential) to the clay-flint of Borderies (which contributes rounder, violet-edged character) — the blend draws on eaux-de-vie matured across 65 chais, with riverside cellars near the Charente producing mellower spirit and drier inland chais yielding more characterful stock. Distillation is performed in traditional Charentais alembic copper pot stills, with Hennessy's house-defining decision to distill both with and without lees, and to reintroduce heads and tails into the brouillis before the second pass — concentrating congeners for a richer, darker-fruited profile than most peers. Eaux-de-vie are aged a minimum of four years in Limousin oak from Hennessy's own forests, cooperaged on-site at 400–500 barrels per year by hand. The Year of the Horse edition alters nothing in the liquid — the collectible packaging isolates this VSOP as a cultural artifact without reformulating the blend, a transparency rare among limited-edition spirits releases. name: Nonino  Grappa Il Merlot (700 ml)
short_description: Nonino's 1973 Monovitigno® revolution reaches Merlot: 100% single-varietal pomace, copper steam stills, Percoto distillery.
description: In 1973, Giannola and Benito Nonino made a decision that rewrote Italian spirits law: they separated individual grape pomaces and distilled each variety alone, creating the Monovitigno® category from scratch — beginning with near-extinct Picolit and eventually extending to Merlot. That single act of transgression transformed grappa from a rustic farmhouse by-product into a terroir-expressive spirit with legal standing. Il Merlot Monovitigno® is distilled from 100% Merlot pomace, sourced fresh and transported to the Percoto distillery in Pavia di Udine within one hour of pressing — a non-negotiable house rule that prevents oxidation and unwanted secondary fermentation in the raw material. Fermented red pomace is distilled immediately in Benito Nonino's bespoke handcrafted copper steam discontinuous stills — batch apparatus that allows precise head-and-tail cuts impossible in continuous column distillation. Aging proceeds in small barriques drawn from Nonino's seven warehouses, which hold 2,620 casks spanning Nevers, Limousin, Grésigne oak, ex-Sherry, ex-Sauternes, Acacia, wild Cherry, Ash, and Pear wood; zero color additives are used across the entire range. What distinguishes this expression within the Monovitigno® lineup is the Merlot pomace itself: red-fermented rather than sweet, it arrives at the still already carrying developed tannin structure and dark-fruit aromatic compounds that survive distillation as a distinctly vinous, fleshy character no white-grape grappa can replicate. As a non-vintage spirit, character is consistent across releases; best appreciated neat at cellar temperature to preserve the aromatic lift from the copper distillation.

name: Nonino  Grappa Il Moscato (700 ml)
short_description: Percoto distillery, 1973: Nonino invented single-varietal grappa with Moscato pomace, copper steam stills, zero color additives.
description: In 1973, Benito and Giannola Nonino made a decision that rewrote Italian spirits law: they separated individual grape pomaces by variety and distilled each alone, beginning with Picolit. The Moscato Monovitigno® followed that same logic — 100% Moscato pomace, freshly destalked and delivered to the Percoto distillery within one hour of pressing, then held under vacuum in stainless-steel tanks at controlled temperature for up to 48 hours to arrest harmful secondary fermentations before distillation. Residual wine-fermentation yeast drives the sugar conversion over approximately five days. Distillation runs through bespoke copper steam discontinuous (batch) stills — among the 66 handcrafted units housed across five artisanal sub-distilleries on the Percoto estate — with precise head-and-tail cuts made by hand. The result is bottled with zero color additives; no colorants are used across any Nonino expression. What makes this unusual within the grappa category is the Monovitigno® concept itself: before Nonino lobbied for its legal recognition, single-varietal pomace distillation did not exist as a protected Italian spirit category. As a non-vintage spirit, character is consistent across releases — expect the aromatic intensity of Moscato's terpene-rich skins translated directly into the distillate, with the freshness preserved by that immediate pomace handling protocol.

name: Giffard  Cherry Brandy Liqueur (700 ml)
short_description: Loire pharmacist-precision: Giffard macerates Morello cherries in sugar-beet neutral spirit, isolating pure fruit essence over confected sweetness.
description: When Émile Giffard founded his liqueur house in Angers in 1885, he applied a trained pharmacist's discipline — isolating botanical essences rather than masking them with sugar — a methodology that still governs every batch produced at the Avrillé facility today. The Loire Valley's agricultural abundance, historically dubbed 'the Garden of France,' supplies the fruit backbone, with 65% of all plant and fruit purchases sourced from French producers. Cherry Brandy Liqueur is built on maceration of cherries in neutral spirit distilled from sugar beet, a process controlled for light, temperature, and oxygen exposure to preserve chromatic intensity and true-to-fruit aroma rather than the jammy, confected character typical of mass-market cherry cordials. The sugar beet base spirit — deliberately neutral — allows the cherry character to dominate without competing oak or grain influence. Each batch is tasted before bottling, passing through assemblage, filtration, and quality control checkpoints that reflect the house's pharmaceutical founding logic. What separates this expression from category peers is Giffard's refusal to engineer sweetness as the primary flavour vector: the maceration targets aromatic fidelity first, yielding a vivid, fruit-forward liqueur where cherry skin bitterness and stone-fruit depth remain legible. Drink as a cocktail modifier or over ice where the cherry's natural acidity can express itself fully.

name: Giffard  Poire Williams (William Pear Liqueur ) (700 ml)
short_description: Loire Valley pharmacist-precision: William pear macerated in sugar-beet neutral spirit at Avrillé, capturing the fruit's full aromatic range without confected sweetness.
description: When Émile Giffard founded his liqueur house in 1885, he applied a trained pharmacist's discipline to flavour extraction — isolating botanical essences with chemist's precision rather than sweetening his way to palatability. That founding logic governs the Poire Williams today. William pears are macerated in neutral spirit distilled from sugar beet, with light, temperature, and oxygen carefully controlled across the steeping period to preserve the fruit's volatile aromatics intact — the same methodology applied to every expression in the Avrillé facility on Avenue de la Violette, near Angers in the Loire Valley, historically France's most productive agricultural corridor. The Loire's moderate, humid microclimate and the house's proximity to French fruit growers — 65% of fruit and plant purchases sourced from French producers — means raw material quality is treated as a production variable, not an afterthought. What distinguishes this expression within the category is Giffard's deliberate refusal to engineer confected sweetness: the maceration targets the pear's full aromatic register, from the green, slightly underripe top notes through to the ripe, honeyed core, rather than flattening it into a single syrupy impression. The result is a fruit liqueur with unusual aromatic fidelity — the kind of precision that makes it as useful as a technical cocktail component as it is served simply chilled.

name: Giffard  Crème De Framboise (Raspberry) (700 ml)
short_description: Loire Valley raspberry liqueur built on pharmacist-precision maceration — Giffard's 1885 chemist's discipline in a vivid, true-to-fruit crème.
description:
When Émile Giffard founded his liqueur house in Angers in 1885, he brought a pharmacist's instinct for isolating pure botanical essences rather than masking ingredients behind sugar. That founding logic governs every batch of Crème de Framboise produced today, now in the hands of fifth-generation siblings operating from Avrillé in the Loire Valley — France's self-styled 'Garden of France' and its second-largest agricultural region.

Raspberries are macerated in neutral spirit distilled from sugar beet, with light, temperature, and oxygen tightly controlled across a steeping window calibrated to the fruit's aromatic volatility. The goal is extraction of the berry's full spectrum — not just sweetness, but the green-leafy top note and the seed-adjacent tartness that distinguish fresh raspberry from confection. Sweetened with sugar beet sugar and filtered before bottling, each batch is tasted for colour and aroma fidelity before release.

What separates this from mass-market raspberry cordials is Giffard's deliberate refusal to engineer toward generic 'red fruit sweetness': the maceration protocol targets the aromatic compound profile of the actual fruit, yielding a colour and flavour that reads as botanical rather than synthetic. The result is a crème with structural tartness intact — functional in cocktails where lesser raspberry liqueurs collapse into syrup.

name: Giffard  Menthe Pastille (White Mint) (700 ml)
short_description: Steam-distilled Black Mitcham peppermint from Avrillé — Émile Giffard's 1885 pharmacist logic applied to mint's purest aromatic core.
description: In 1885, trained pharmacist Émile Giffard applied a chemist's discipline to liqueur production — not to sweeten, but to isolate. Menthe-Pastille is the direct expression of that founding logic: rather than macerating mint leaves in neutral spirit, Giffard deploys steam distillation of Black Mitcham peppermint (Mentha × piperita), liberating essential oils into a crystal-clear distillate that captures the botanical's volatile aromatic compounds without the vegetal weight maceration would introduce. The base spirit is neutral spirit distilled from sugar beet, lightly sweetened with beet sugar — a Loire Valley agricultural choice that keeps the palate clean and lets the mint distillate dominate without competing sweetness. What separates this from mass-market crème de menthe is the absence of confected, candy-register flavour: steam distillation yields a distillate closer to the raw herb's essential oil profile — cooling, camphoraceous, and precise — rather than the synthetic peppermint extract common in lower-tier cordials. Each batch passes a tasting panel before bottling, with filtration as standard. The result is a white mint liqueur of unusual aromatic fidelity, built for cocktail precision as much as digestif use.

name: Giffard  Fleur De Sureau Sauvage (Elder flower) (700 ml)
short_description: Loire Valley elderflower liqueur from a pharmacist-founded house; wild-flower maceration in sugar-beet spirit captures fleeting spring blossom at its aromatic peak.
description: When Émile Giffard opened his liqueur house in Avrillé in 1885, he applied a pharmacist's discipline to botanical extraction — isolating each ingredient's purest aromatic essence rather than building sweetness around it. That founding logic governs Fleur de Sureau Sauvage to this day, now in the hands of fifth-generation siblings operating from the same Loire Valley facility. The Loire's designation as le Jardin de la France is not merely poetic: the region's temperate, river-moderated microclimate produces the wild elderflower whose fleeting spring bloom defines this expression. Maceration in neutral spirit distilled from sugar beet — Giffard's house base — steeps the blossoms under controlled light, temperature, and oxygen conditions to arrest oxidation and lock in the volatile muscat-grape-like floral compounds that evaporate within days of harvest. The result is a liqueur whose colour and aroma read as true-to-source rather than confected, a direct consequence of the house's refusal to compensate with added flavouring agents. What separates this from the category's dominant elderflower cordial tradition is the extraction medium itself: ethanol captures fat-soluble aromatic compounds that water-based cordials cannot, yielding a textural density and aromatic persistence no syrup achieves. Drink over ice or in a spritz where the blossom character can open without dilution masking its precision.

name: Kozaemon  Shiroku Junmai Kabosu Sake (1.8 L)
short_description: Kabosu-infused Junmai from Kozaemon's 1702 Mizunami brewery — umakuchi philosophy meets citrus-spiked rice umami in a 1.8 L magnum format.
description:
Founded in 1702 by Nakashima Kozaemon in Mizunami City, Gifu, this brewery has spent over three centuries drawing water from an underground spring fed by snowmelt off Mt. Byobu — a soft, mineral-light source that underpins the house's signature umakuchi style: rich, umami-forward, and deliberately opposed to the light-dry tanrei karakuchi mainstream.

Mizunami sits at 70% forest cover in the cool, humid Mino highlands adjacent to the Kiso River watershed — conditions that slow fermentation and concentrate rice-derived amino acids. Kozaemon amplifies this further through 98%-humidity koji cultivation to maximise enzyme activity and a four-stage yodan fermentation (a fourth rice-and-water addition beyond the standard three-stage sandan shikomi), extracting umami depth that three-stage brewing cannot reach.

This Shiroku expression introduces kabosu — the tart, aromatic Japanese citrus native to Oita Prefecture — into a no-added-alcohol Junmai base, a transgression within the sake category that reframes the liquid as a culinary ingredient rather than a standalone beverage. The 1.8 L magnum format (isshobin) is the traditional Japanese hospitality vessel, here repositioned for table-side sharing. The kabosu's sharp yuzu-adjacent acidity cuts through the brewery's characteristic umami weight, creating a tension that is structural rather than decorative.

name: Bijofu  Yuzu Shuwa (500 ml)
short_description: Yuzu sparkling sake from Tano-cho's nansui brewery — secondary in-bottle fermentation, CEL-24 yeast, live citrus from Kochi's own groves.
description: Hamakawa Shoten, founded in 1904 on a coastal strip between the Pacific and the Yanase cedar mountains in Tano-cho, built its reputation on brewing with ultra-soft nansui water drawn from the subterranean Nahari River — a water type once considered incompatible with quality sake production. Yuzu Shuwa (柚子シュワ) extends that philosophy into sparkling form, blending Bijofu's Ginjo sake base with yuzu juice sourced from Kochi Prefecture, where yuzu cultivation is a regional identity. The sake base is fermented using Kochi's proprietary CEL-24 yeast, one of Japan's most aromatic brewing strains, generating apple and citrus-forward isoamyl acetate compounds that amplify rather than compete with the yuzu's pithy, floral character. Secondary in-bottle fermentation — the same méthode used in Bijofu's sparkling Junmai Daiginjo — builds fine, persistent effervescence at 13% ABV, keeping the palate weight low and the yuzu's volatile aromatics intact. What sets this apart categorically: the carbonation is not force-injected but yeast-generated, giving a texture closer to pét-nat than to commercial sparkling sake. The nansui water's near-zero mineral hardness strips away any roughness, leaving the yuzu's bitter-citrus edge as the sole structural anchor — tart, clean, and unpadded.

name: Suntory  Yamazaki Cask Umeshu (750 ml)
short_description: Yamazaki whisky casks repurposed to macerate Japanese ume plums — Suntory's distillery site lends its soft river-aquifer water to a liqueur built on single malt infrastructure.
description:
At the misty confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers — the same soft-water aquifer that Sen no Rikyū drew from for his tea ceremonies — Suntory's Yamazaki Distillery has produced malt whisky since 1923. Yamazaki Cask Umeshu repurposes those same single malt casks as maceration vessels for ume plums, meaning every batch carries the aromatic residue of American oak, ex-bourbon, or sherry wood that shaped the distillery's whisky before it shaped this liqueur.


The Yamazaki site's warm, humid microclimate — which accelerates whisky maturation faster than any other major Japanese distillery — acts on the ume maceration in kind, drawing stone-fruit intensity from the plums while the whisky-seasoned wood contributes layered vanilla, dried fig, and faint sandalwood. The soft, low-mineral water drawn from the river-fed aquifer at roughly 25 m elevation integrates the plum extract without hardness or astringency.


The defining transgression here: where most commercial umeshu is produced in neutral spirit or shochu, this expression is built inside casks with a documented single malt history — the wood is not a neutral container but an active flavour contributor carrying the Yamazaki house character into the liqueur format. No filtration or production parameters are officially disclosed for this expression. Drink now or cellar short-term; the cask influence will continue to evolve in bottle.

name: Suntory  Prucia Plum Liquer de France (720 ml)
short_description: Suntory's plum liqueur crafted in France, blending Japanese ume tradition with French eau-de-vie base — a deliberate East-West production transgression.
description: Suntory — the Osaka house founded by Shinjirō Torii in 1899 and the force that introduced Japanese whisky to the world — has long operated outside categorical orthodoxy. Prucia extends that instinct into liqueur: a Japanese-style plum liqueur produced in France, marrying the ume-liqueur tradition of Japan with a French fruit eau-de-vie base and French plums, rather than the domestic Japanese ume fruit used in conventional umeshu. The result sits in a category of one — neither a standard umeshu nor a European plum brandy, but a deliberate hybrid that Suntory engineered to carry the aromatic profile of ripe plum with the structural lift of a French spirit foundation. Bottled at a moderate ABV suited to aperitif and digestif service, Prucia is produced under Suntory's liqueur operations, which now include the Osaka Spirits & Liqueurs Craft Distillery (opened 2024–2025). The liqueur's special feature is its Franco-Japanese production logic: French plums and a French base spirit are processed under Japanese quality protocols, producing a sweetness calibration and aromatic clarity that diverges sharply from both European mirabelle liqueurs and traditional Japanese umeshu. No age statement applies; the expression is non-vintage and designed for consistent house style across releases.

name: Suntory  Yamazaki Cask Umeshu Plum (750 ml)
short_description: Yamazaki whisky casks repurposed to macerate Japanese ume plums — a liqueur born at Japan's first malt distillery, where river-fed soft water shaped a century of maturation.
description: Shinjirō Torii built Japan's first malt whisky distillery in 1923 at the misty confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers — the same soft, mineral-light water historically drawn by tea master Sen no Rikyū. That water, and the whisky casks it helped season over decades, now form the backbone of Yamazaki Cask Umeshu. Ume plums are macerated directly in Yamazaki single malt whisky casks, meaning the fruit absorbs residual malt spirit, oak tannin, and whatever cask character — American white oak, ex-bourbon, or sherry — those barrels previously held. The result is categorically distinct from standard shochu-base umeshu: the plum's tartaric acidity and stone-fruit flesh are threaded through a whisky-oak scaffold rather than a neutral spirit base. Produced at ~25 m elevation in Shimamoto-cho, Osaka Prefecture, where the warm, humid microclimate that accelerates whisky maturation also intensifies the maceration of the ume. The defining transgression here is the cask itself — using spent Yamazaki barrels as the maceration vessel is a direct extension of Suntory's 'Tsukuriwake' philosophy of extracting maximum flavour diversity from a single site's infrastructure. No critic scores are available for this expression; serve lightly chilled or over a single large ice cube.

name: Plantation  Stiggins Fancy Pineapple Rum (700 ml)
short_description: Victoria pineapple rinds macerated in aged dark rum, flesh in white rum — blended and finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks, Charentes.
description: Alexandre Gabriel's Stiggins' Fancy Pineapple is built on a split-maceration architecture borrowed from the liqueur tradition: Victoria pineapple rinds — the aromatic, terpene-rich outer skin — are steeped in aged dark rum, while the flesh is macerated separately in white rum, preserving the bright, acidic interior fruit character. The two macerates are then blended and finished in ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks at Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes — the same continental cellar where Plantation's double-aged rums complete their maturation. That Cognac-cask finish is the structural move: it adds dried-fruit depth and baking-spice tannin that a simple pineapple infusion never achieves. What makes this expression categorically unusual is the deliberate separation of rind from flesh at maceration — a technique that treats pineapple the way a perfumer treats a botanical, extracting aromatic compounds and sugar-acid structure independently before assembly. The result sits between a flavoured rum and a liqueur in density, with the Cognac-cask finish preventing the pineapple from reading as confected. Drink now through the near term; the fruit character is at its most vivid within two to three years of bottling.

name: Plantation  O.F.T.D Overproof Dark Rum (700 ml)
short_description: Barbados column still + Jamaican high-ester pot still + Guyanese pot still, blended and bottled at 69% ABV — the overproof benchmark of Plantation's double-ageing house.
description: O.F.T.D. — Old Fashioned Traditional Dark — was built as a deliberate provocation: a 69% ABV overproof blend designed to restore the raw, high-proof character that 18th-century naval and plantation rums carried before commercial dilution became standard. The blend draws on three distinct distillate characters: Barbados column still rum from Stade's West Indies Rum Distillery (WIRD) at Blackrock, St. Michael — a coral-limestone coastal site whose seafront warehouses impart a saline maritime edge — paired with Jamaican high-ester pot still rum from Long Pond and/or Clarendon (both co-owned via National Rums of Jamaica), and a Guyanese pot still component for deep molasses backbone. Each origin undergoes 1–2 years of tropical ageing in ex-bourbon American oak, where the Caribbean angel's share runs 6–7% annually, concentrating the distillate rapidly. The blend then receives a 3–6 month finish in French oak vats at Château de Bonbonnet, Charentes, Maison Ferrand's continental cellar, adding a faint tannin frame without softening the proof. What no peer does: OFTD is bottled at 69% ABV without chill filtration, preserving the full ester load of the Jamaican marks — a proof level that makes it simultaneously a sipping overproof and a cocktail-grade powerhouse. Drink now or hold; the high ABV ensures stability indefinitely.

Plantation  Xaymaca Special Dry Rum (700 ml)
short_description: Jamaican pot-still high-ester rum, double-aged tropically then finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks at Château de Bonbonnet, Charentes — bottled dry.
description: Plantation's Xaymaca Special Dry takes its name from the Taíno word for Jamaica, anchoring the liquid firmly to the island's two great pot-still houses: Long Pond (Clark's Town, north coast) and Clarendon Distillers Ltd (Lionel Town, south coast), both co-owned by Maison Ferrand through National Rums of Jamaica. These are high-ester pot-still distillates — the product of slow, open fermentation that generates the volatile esters responsible for Jamaica's signature overripe-fruit intensity. Stage one maturation takes place tropically in ex-bourbon American oak, where the Caribbean climate drives an angel's share of 6–7% per year, concentrating the distillate far faster than any European cellar could. Stage two moves the rum to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, France, where ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks layer dried-fruit and baking-spice structure over the tropical core — reviving the 17th–19th century practice of finishing Caribbean rum in European port cellars. The defining transgression here is the 'Special Dry' designation: unlike most Plantation expressions, Xaymaca receives no Cognac-style dosage, leaving the high-ester Jamaican character completely unmasked and the finish bone-dry. The result is a rum that reads more like an uncompromising single-terroir statement than a blended house style — all funk, stone fruit, and raw oak tannin with nothing softened.

name: Plantation  Isle of Fiji Rum (700 ml)
short_description: Rum Co. of Fiji distillate, 8–15 yrs tropical aging, finished in ex-Ferrand Cognac casks at Château de Bonbonnet, Charentes — double-aged across two hemispheres.
description: Alexandre Gabriel's Plantation project revived a practice dormant since the 19th century: shipping Caribbean rum to European cellars for a second maturation, mirroring the dock-ageing tradition once common in Bristol and Bordeaux. The Isle of Fiji expression sources its distillate from the Rum Co. of Fiji, using a sugarcane-derived base fermented and distilled on the volcanic South Pacific island before beginning its two-stage journey. Stage one sees the spirit mature tropically for 8–15 years in American white oak ex-bourbon barrels, where the tropical climate drives an angel's share of 6–7% annually — concentrating the distillate at a rate more than double what French cellars would achieve. Stage two moves the rum to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, where ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac casks layer dried-fruit depth and baking-spice structure onto the tropical core. The result carries the maritime and volcanic character of a South Pacific terroir that no Barbados or Jamaican expression in the Plantation range can replicate — Fiji's cane-derived base and equatorial fermentation profile produce a distinctly floral, grassy register absent from molasses-driven peers. Select expressions also receive Plantation's Cognac-style dosage: toasted brown sugar aged one year in Ferrand Cognac barrels, added as a flavour-enhancing syrup rather than a blunt sweetener. As a non-vintage blend, drinking window is open now.

name: Plantation Trinidad 2016 "Single Cask" 2023 Rum (700 ml)
short_description: Single cask Trinidad 2016, column-distilled, double-aged tropically then in ex-Ferrand Cognac oak at Château de Bonbonnet — non-chill-filtered at cask strength.
description: Plantation's Single Cask series isolates what the double-ageing method does to a single barrel rather than a blend — a deliberate act of transparency from Alexandre Gabriel's Maison Ferrand, which also openly discloses its Cognac-style dosage practice in an industry that rarely does. This Trinidad distillate originates from continuous column still distillation, the island's signature technique, producing a cleaner, more precise spirit than the pot-still marks Plantation sources from Barbados and Jamaica. Stage one tropical ageing in ex-bourbon American oak ran from distillation in 2016 under Trinidad's equatorial heat, where the angel's share accelerates concentration at roughly 6–7% per year. The cask then travelled to Château de Bonbonnet, Ars, Charentes, France, for continental finishing in ex-Ferrand French oak Cognac barrels — the cooler cellar slowing extraction and layering dried-fruit and baking-spice structure onto the tropical base before bottling in 2023 at natural cask strength, non-chill-filtered. What distinguishes this expression within the Plantation portfolio is its single-cask isolation of Trinidad's column-still character — a terroir profile that disappears entirely in the brand's blended expressions where it plays a supporting, structural role. The seven-year dual-climate maturation window suggests the rum is drinking at full development now, with no need to hold.

Kirinzan Mellow (720 ml)
short_description: Niigata Tanrei Karakuchi from the foot of Mt. Kirinzan — soft Tokonami River water, G901 yeast, slow cold fermentation, 100% locally grown rice.
description:
When the Saito family renamed their brewery Kirinzan in 1882, they were staking an identity on place — the forested mountain rising behind the brewery in Aga-machi, Higashi-kanbara-gun. Nearly 30 years of direct collaboration with 55 local growers later, the brewery achieved what almost no Japanese sake house of comparable scale has managed: 100% locally sourced rice, supported by an in-house agricultural division established in 2011. Mellow is the expression of that commitment at the everyday table.

The brewery draws soft, low-mineral water from the Tokonami River basin, filtered through beech-forest soils — water chemistry that suppresses aggressive fermentation and enables the extended, slow, cold fermentation that defines Niigata's Tanrei Karakuchi (light and dry) school. The surrounding landscape is 94% dense forest; the microclimate is cold and stable, ideal for unhurried koji work.

Fermentation uses proprietary yeast G901 and a sokujo moto starter, with Gohyaku-mangoku rice — Niigata's signature variety — milled to 65% seimaibuai alongside Koshi-ibuki. Tank-aged in stainless steel and pasteurized before bottling at 15–16% ABV; not genshu.

Where most breweries treat entry-level futsushu as commodity, Kirinzan deliberately positions Mellow as a serious terroir expression — same water, same rice origin, same cold-fermentation discipline as the Junmai Daiginjo tier. The result is a dry, clean sake with a delicate umami backbone and a razor-sharp finish that performs across a wide temperature range.

name: Kirinzan Extra Dry (720 ml)
short_description: Niigata's Tanrei Karakuchi archetype — soft Tokonami River water, Gohyakumangoku rice, G901 yeast, razor-dry finish from a brewery founded in 1843.
description:
When the Saito family pivoted from charcoal manufacturing to sake brewing in 1843 at the foot of Mt. Kirinzan in Aga-machi, they inherited one decisive natural asset: exceptionally soft water drawn from the Tokonami River basin, filtered through dense beech-forest soils. That low-mineral water profile is the technical engine behind the Extra Dry's character — soft water suppresses fermentation speed, enabling the extended cold fermentation that defines Niigata's Tanrei Karakuchi (light-and-dry) school.

The rice is Gohyakumangoku, grown within 10 km of the brewery by a network of 55 local Aga-machi farmers — a 100% local-sourcing achievement that took nearly three decades to realize and is now managed by Kirinzan's own in-house agricultural division. Milled to 65% seimaibuai, fermented with proprietary yeast G901 via sokujo moto starter, and tank-aged in stainless steel, the sake is pasteurized before bottling at 15–16% ABV — no genshu, no wood contact.

What sets the Extra Dry apart within even Kirinzan's own lineup is its deliberate positioning at the sharpest end of the SMV spectrum: where the Junmai Ginjo tier still carries a delicate umami cushion, the Extra Dry strips the finish to near-austerity, making it one of the most food-aggressive expressions in the Niigata portfolio. NV by nature; drink now or hold short-term, served anywhere from 5 °C (chilled) to 50 °C (kan) without loss of structure.
