name: Remy Martin X.O. Night Step Into The Light (Limited Edition)
short_description: Fine Champagne XO blending up to 400 eaux-de-vie from deep chalky Grande & Petite Champagne soils — lees-distilled, Limousin-aged 10–30+ years.
description:
Rémy Martin's founding commitment — made in 1724 by a Charente vintner — to source exclusively from Grande and Petite Champagne has never been diluted: every drop in this bottle qualifies as Cognac Fine Champagne AOC, a restriction most rival houses reserve only for prestige cuvées. The XO Night Step Into The Light is a limited-edition expression of that same terroir logic, dressed in a bottle designed for nocturnal ceremony without altering the liquid inside.

The chalk runs deep here. Grande and Petite Champagne soils are calcareous to their core — permeable enough to drain, yet moisture-retentive enough to sustain Ugni Blanc through dry summers, preserving the high acidity that survives double distillation and fuels decades of aromatic development in barrel. No other Cognac cru produces this combination of tension and longevity.

The house's defining technical signature is distillation on the lees — the residual yeast sediment most houses rack away before the still fires. Rémy Martin retains it, running each charentais copper pot still cycle for approximately 24 hours across two passes, extracting a richer, more aromatic new-make. The XO blend draws on up to 400 individually selected eaux-de-vie, the youngest aged a minimum of 10 years, the oldest exceeding 30, all matured in French Limousin oak from trees at least a century old, seasoned outdoors 2–3 years before coopering.

What sets this expression apart within the XO tier is the limited-edition packaging format — a collector's object that makes the house's most accessible prestige cognac a deliberate gifting and display artefact — while the liquid itself remains the full Fine Champagne blend, unchanged.

No vintage applies; as a non-vintage AOC blend, drinking window is open now and extends indefinitely once sealed.

name: Philippe Pacalet  Marc de Bourgogne (500 ml)
short_description: Marc de Bourgogne from Philippe Pacalet — Burgundy's natural-wine iconoclast turns pomace into spirit, distilled from zero-sulphur, whole-cluster Pinot Noir and Chardonnay marc.
description: Philippe Pacalet — nephew of Marcel Lapierre and intellectual heir to Jules Chauvet, the scientist who founded the theoretical basis of natural winemaking — built his Beaune négociant in 2001 on a single principle: remove every intervention that obscures terroir. That same philosophy extends to this Marc de Bourgogne, distilled from the pomace of wines made with no added sulphur during vinification, native-yeast fermentation, and zero fining or filtration. The raw material is therefore unusually clean: whole-cluster-pressed Chardonnay and whole-cluster-fermented Pinot Noir marc from clay-limestone and marl plots across the Côte de Nuits and Côte de Beaune, the absence of sulphur preserving the aromatic integrity of the grape solids before distillation. No production parameters (still type, distillation cut, cask regime, or age statement) are confirmed by official Pacalet documentation, but the house's unfined, unfiltered, minimal-intervention philosophy is the defining lens through which this spirit should be understood. The special feature here is the raw material itself: marc sourced exclusively from no-sulphur, organically farmed Burgundian fruit — a provenance virtually no commercial marc producer can claim. As an NV expression, drinking window is open now, best served at cellar temperature to allow volatile aromatics to integrate.

name: Coutanseaux Aîné  Hors d'Age Cognac (700 ml)
short_description: Dormant since the 20th century, revived in 2015 with stocks from 1916: Grande Champagne Hors d'âge blending 40–120 years of chalk-driven eaux-de-vie.
description:
Founded in 1767 by Louis Coutanseaux as a cooperage in Saintes, the house spent most of the 20th century dormant — its revival in 2015 by Guillaume and Rodolphe Frerejean Taittinger was made possible only because century-old reserve stocks had survived intact, some dating to 1916. That continuity of oak time is the entire argument of this Hors d'âge.

Every eau-de-vie in the blend originates exclusively from Grande Champagne, Premier Cru de Cognac — the highest-chalk subsoil of all six crus, where chalk, clay, and limestone converge to produce distillates of exceptional finesse and structural tension that reward extreme aging far beyond what sandier crus can sustain.

Distillation is performed sur lies in traditional copper alambics charentais, a deliberate choice that loads the base spirit with aromatic and organoleptic density before a single day in oak. The Hors d'âge then draws on French oak reserves aged a minimum of 40 years, extending to 120 years, yielding pronounced rancio, silky tannin architecture, and deep floral-spice complexity that the house's cooper origins treat as the primary artistic medium.

What no peer does: Coutanseaux Aîné offers no VS, VSOP, or standard-age expression — the Hors d'âge is the house's raison d'être, bottled in a hand-blown, individually unique glass decanter, making each 70cl a singular object. The drinking window is open now and extends indefinitely once decanted with care.

name: Coutanseaux Aîné XO Cognac (700 ml)
short_description: Grande Champagne XO from a house dormant for a century, revived in 2015 on 15–25-year eaux-de-vie distilled sur lies from chalk-rich Premier Cru soils.
description:
Founded in 1767 by Louis Coutanseaux as a cooperage in Saintes, the house spent most of the 20th century dormant before Guillaume and Rodolphe Frerejean Taittinger — descendants of the Taittinger and Rouyer-Guillet lines — revived it in 2015 using century-old reserve stocks. That cooper lineage is not incidental: prolonged oak maturation is the house's primary artistic medium, not an afterthought.

Every eaux-de-vie in the XO blend originates exclusively from Grande Champagne, Premier Cru de Cognac — the cru with the highest chalk concentration among all six Cognac sub-regions. That deep chalk-and-limestone subsoil drives exceptional finesse and longevity in the distillate, producing a structural tension between floral delicacy and oxidative depth that lower-chalk crus cannot replicate.

Distillation is performed sur lies in traditional copper alambics charentais — a deliberate choice that extracts greater organoleptic richness from the lees before the double-distillation pass. The XO blends eaux-de-vie aged 15–25 years in French oak, well beyond the legal XO minimum, yielding pronounced rancio character alongside dried citrus peel, walnut, and candied ginger.

The house's most transgressive decision: no VS, VSOP, or young-aged expression exists in the range — the XO is the entry point, positioning Coutanseaux Aîné entirely outside the volume-driven Cognac market. The 40% ABV bottling delivers the age-driven complexity without dilution of the rancio signature.

name: Martell Noblige Flame (1.5 L)
short_description: Borderies-dominant Cognac from the house that distills exclusively from lees-free wines — cecillus oak shapes every aromatic layer in this 1.5L Flame format.
description: Founded in 1715 by Jean Martell, the house made one deliberate break from every rival: distilling exclusively from clear, lees-free wines before a drop enters the still — a practice that strips the distillate of its raw character and places the entire burden of flavour-building on the barrel. The result is a cognac whose identity is almost entirely an oak story. Borderies silica-clay soils — the smallest of Cognac's six crus, with Martell purchasing 50–60% of its entire output — yield eaux-de-vie of distinctive violet and candied-fruit character that no other cru replicates. The Gallienne Distillery in the heart of Borderies runs copper alambic Charentais pot stills at ~130 hL capacity, the largest première chauffe stills in Cognac, with gas pressure adjusted hourly along the proprietary 'Martell Curve' to protect the clean heart cut. Aging proceeds exclusively in fine-grained cecillus (Tronçais-style) French oak, lightly toasted at minimum 17–20 minutes bousinage, in a mix of new and seasoned 'red' barrels; once peak maturity is reached, eaux-de-vie are transferred to glass demijohns to arrest further development — a precision arrest no AOC rule requires. The 1.5L Flame format presents Noblige, a blend drawing on all four crus with a Borderies core, at 40% ABV, rested in vat 1–4 months before bottling. Drink now through the mid-2030s; the magnum format slows oxidative exchange, preserving the floral lift longer than a standard 70cl.

name: Baron De Sigognac Carafe 25 Ans  (700 ml)
short_description: Bas-Armagnac's rarest substrate: 25 years on sables fauves, Baco-driven fruit depth from Château de Bordeneuve's 1921 alembic.
description: At Château de Bordeneuve near Eauze, father-and-son Jean-Claude and Thomas Guasch operate one of Armagnac's last fully vertically integrated estates — vineyard, 1921 copper alembic, ageing cellars, and bottling line under a single roof, a configuration that has become genuinely rare in the appellation. The estate sits on sables fauves, the tawny sandy soils exclusive to Bas-Armagnac, which drain rapidly and stress the vine into producing low-yield, aromatic fruit rather than bulk. That soil signature translates directly into the distillate: lighter mineral structure, earlier aromatic development, and a fruit persistence that distinguishes Bas-Armagnac from the heavier, clay-dominant Ténarèze sub-appellation. The 25 Ans Carafe is built on a 50/50 blend of Ugni Blanc and Baco — the latter a hybrid variety now largely restricted to Armagnac, deliberately retained by the Guasch family for the richness and dark-fruit depth it contributes. Distillation runs through the traditional alembic armagnacais (continuous column still) to approximately 56% ABV, a single-pass method that preserves far more congeners than Cognac's double distillation. After a quarter-century in French oak (Limousin and Vosges forest), the spirit develops the prized rancio character — oxidative, walnut-skin depth — while Baco's fruit core remains intact. The Carafe format signals a deliberate presentation choice: a blended, age-statement expression rather than a single vintage, allowing the cellar master to maintain stylistic consistency across releases. Drink now through the next decade.

name: Bas Armagnac Laberdolive 1946 Domaine De Jaurrey (700 ml)
Baron De Sigognac 10 ans (700 ml)
short_description: Bas-Armagnac from sables fauves soils near Eauze — 50% Baco hybrid, single alembic distillation, 10 years in French oak.
description: At Château de Bordeneuve, the Guasch family operates one of Armagnac's rarest vertically integrated estates — vineyards, a 1921 copper alembic armagnacais, ageing cellars, and bottling line all under one roof, a configuration that gives Jean-Claude and Thomas Guasch complete control over every aromatic decision. That 1921 continuous still is the technical key: a single-pass distillation at approximately 56% ABV preserves far more congeners than Cognac's double-distillation method, locking in the raw aromatic material that a decade of oak will slowly resolve. The estate sits on sables fauves — the tawny sandy soils that define Bas-Armagnac's most prestigious terroir near Eauze — which drain freely, stress the vines gently, and consistently yield fruit-forward, texturally supple distillates. The 50% Baco / 50% Ugni Blanc blend is a deliberate transgression: Baco, a hybrid variety now largely restricted to Armagnac, delivers the house's signature richness and depth of fruit, while Ugni Blanc contributes structural acidity. After 10 years in French oak (Limousin and Vosges forest cited for estate expressions), the spirit reaches 40% ABV with dried stone fruit, toasted oak, and the early emergence of rancio character. The use of Baco — increasingly rare as producers shift to all-Ugni Blanc — is what separates this fruit profile from any Cognac or Ténarèze counterpart. Approachable now, with the structure to develop further over several years in bottle.

name: Baron De Sigognac 20 ans (700 ml)
short_description: Bas-Armagnac's rarest hybrid: 50% Baco on sables fauves soils, single-pass alembic at 56% ABV, 20 years in Limousin/Vosges oak.
description: The Guasch family's Château de Bordeneuve near Eauze has matured Armagnac in medieval cellars with beaten-earth floors — a passive humidity-control system that no modern warehouse replicates. The 20 Ans d'Âge is built on 50% Baco / 50% Ugni Blanc, a deliberate retention of the hybrid Baco grape that most Armagnac houses have quietly abandoned; Baco's thick skins and high natural sugar load drive the expression's characteristic density of dried fruit and grip. Château de Bordeneuve's sables fauves — the tawny sandy soils exclusive to Bas-Armagnac — drain rapidly, stressing the vine and concentrating aromatic precursors before harvest. Distillation runs through the estate's 1921 copper alembic armagnacais in a single continuous pass to approximately 56% ABV, a cut point that retains far more congeners than Cognac's double-distilled output. The spirit then rests a minimum of 20 years in 400–420-litre French oak (Limousin and Vosges forest) within those same cellars, developing rancio without the aggressive tannin extraction that smaller barrels would impose. What sets this expression apart is vertical integration so complete — vineyard, still, cellar, and bottling line under one roof — that no single stage is contracted out, an operational rarity in Armagnac. Bottled at 40% ABV, the 20 Ans shows the mature, oxidative depth of long oak contact while Baco's fruit architecture keeps the finish alive.

name: Baron De Sigognac CARAFE 50 ans (700 ml)
short_description: 50-year Bas-Armagnac from Château de Bordeneuve's sables fauves soils — Baco/Ugni Blanc, single alembic distillation, half a century in French oak.
description:
Château de Bordeneuve has matured each vintage year separately in its medieval cellars — some with beaten-earth floors — since the Guasch family first committed to estate-controlled production across every stage from vine to bottle. The 50 Ans CARAFE is the product of that unbroken chain: a blend drawn from the estate's deepest reserves, aged no fewer than five decades in the same cellars where the oldest stocks trace back to 1893.

The estate sits near Eauze on sables fauves — the tawny sandy soils that define Bas-Armagnac AOC's most prestigious terroir. These free-draining, low-fertility sands stress the vine into producing concentrated, aromatic fruit while yielding distillates of unusual finesse, structurally lighter than the clay-heavy Ténarèze to the east.

Distillation is performed on-site through a copper alembic armagnacais dating to 1921, a single-pass continuous still that exits at approximately 56% ABV — far lower than Cognac's double-distillation output — preserving a dense congener load that sustains complexity across decades of oak contact. The blend draws on the house's signature 50% Baco / 50% Ugni Blanc split: Baco, a hybrid now largely restricted to Armagnac, delivers the layered dried-fruit depth; Ugni Blanc maintains structural tension. Maturation proceeds in 400–420 litre French oak barrels of Limousin and Vosges forest origin.

After fifty years, the rancio character — that prized oxidative, walnut-oil signature — is fully integrated, a development almost no age-statement Armagnac at this price tier can replicate outside single-vintage library releases.

The drinking window is open now; further cellaring is a matter of personal preference rather than necessity.

name: Baron De Sigognac CARAFE 75 ans (700 ml)
short_description: 75-year Bas-Armagnac from Château de Bordeneuve's sables fauves soils — single-estate, Baco-driven, distilled on a 1921 copper alembic.
description: At Château de Bordeneuve near Eauze, the Guasch family controls every stage of production — vineyard, 1921 copper alembic armagnacais, medieval cellar, and bottling line — a vertical integration rare even within Bas-Armagnac. The estate's sables fauves (tawny sandy soils) are the defining substrate of the sub-appellation: low-fertility, well-drained, they push vines to concentrate aromatic compounds while yielding distillates of unusual finesse and fruit persistence. At 75 years of age, the spirit has spent decades in the estate's beaten-earth-floored cellars, whose stable microclimate governs the slow, even exchange between spirit and Limousin and Vosges forest oak (400–420 litre barrels). The alembic armagnacais produces a single-pass distillate at approximately 56% ABV — far lower than Cognac's double-distillation output — preserving a density of congeners and aromatic complexity that seven and a half decades of oak then sculpt into rancio. The blend anchors on 50% Baco, a hybrid grape now largely restricted to Armagnac, whose structural richness and deep fruit character no all-Ugni Blanc house can replicate. What is exceptional here: 75 years of uninterrupted single-estate maturation, bottled to order so the spirit continues ageing until the last possible moment before release. At this age, dried stone fruit, candied orange, deep spice, and the oxidative walnut-and-mushroom signature of true rancio converge into a finish of extraordinary persistence.

name: Baron De Sigognac GRAND XO PLATINUM (700 ml)
short_description: Château de Bordeneuve's Grand XO Platinum: Bas-Armagnac's tawny-sandy soils, a 1921 copper alembic, and Baco's irreplaceable fruit depth.
description: The Guasch family's Château de Bordeneuve near Eauze — whose medieval cellars predate most of France's great wine estates — distils on a copper alembic armagnacais installed in 1921, a continuous-column still that produces a single-pass distillate at approximately 56% ABV, far lower than Cognac's double-distillation output and deliberately richer in congeners. That technical choice is the foundation of everything Baron de Sigognac does. The estate's tawny-sandy soils (sables fauves) of Bas-Armagnac — the sub-appellation's most prized substrate — drain freely and stress the vine just enough to concentrate aromatic precursors without sacrificing the region's characteristic fruit lift. Maturation proceeds in French oak barrels of 400–420 litres (Limousin and Vosges forest oak cited for estate expressions), the traditional Armagnac format that allows slower, more even oxidative exchange than the smaller barriques of Cognac. The Grand XO Platinum's blend draws on the house's signature 50% Ugni Blanc / 50% Baco formula — Baco being a hybrid grape now largely restricted to Armagnac, contributing a density of dried fruit and spice that all-Ugni Blanc houses simply cannot replicate. What sets this expression apart within the XO tier is the estate's full vertical integration: vineyard, distillery, ageing cellar, and bottling line under one roof, with reserves in the 'Paradise' cellar stretching back to 1893. The Grand XO Platinum draws on those deep reserves for its blending backbone, delivering a long, rancio-touched finish that reflects decades of slow Gascon oak contact.

name: Nakano BC  Kishu Akai Umeshu (1.8 L)
Kozaemon  Junmai Umeshu (500 ml)
short_description: Mizunami spring-water umeshu built on Kozaemon's junmai base — umakuchi philosophy meets ume fruit in a 322-year-old Gifu brewery.
description:
Founded in 1702 by Nakashima Kozaemon in the mountain town of Mizunami, Gifu, this brewery has spent over three centuries drawing water from an underground spring fed by snowmelt off Mt. Byobu — and that same mineral-clean source underpins this umeshu. Mizunami sits at 70% forest cover in the Mino highlands, its cool, humid microclimate slowing fermentation and concentrating rice-derived character before the ume fruit is ever introduced.

The junmai base is built without any distilled alcohol addition, fermented using yamahai starter and four-stage (yodan) brewing additions — a fourth rice-and-water charge beyond the standard three-stage sandan shikomi — which amplifies umami extraction and textural weight beyond what conventional umeshu bases achieve. Koji is cultivated at approximately 98% humidity to maximise enzyme activity, producing a richer, more amino-acid-dense substrate for the ume maceration.

What separates this from category peers is the deliberate umakuchi philosophy: where most commercial umeshu leans on sugar-forward simplicity, Kozaemon's junmai foundation introduces savory depth and natural acidity that keeps the ume fruit taut rather than cloying. The result is a 500 ml expression where stone-fruit sweetness is anchored by genuine rice umami — a structural choice almost no umeshu producer makes explicit.

name: Kozaemon  Junmai Umeshu (1.8 L)
short_description: Junmai-base umeshu from a 1702 Gifu brewery: Mt. Byobu snowmelt water, yamahai starter, umakuchi philosophy in a 1.8 L format.
description:
Founded in 1702 by Nakashima Kozaemon in the mountain town of Mizunami, Gifu, this brewery has spent three centuries drawing water from an underground spring fed by snowmelt off Mt. Byobu (Byobuzan) — and that same mineral-laden, cold-temperature water underpins every expression, including this Junmai Umeshu. Mizunami sits in the forested Mino highlands, where 70% forest cover and a cool, humid microclimate slow fermentation naturally, concentrating rice-derived compounds before the ume fruit is ever introduced.

The Junmai base is produced without any distilled alcohol addition — a legal and philosophical commitment to pure rice fermentation. Kozaemon's house technique of 98%-humidity koji cultivation maximises enzyme activity and amplifies umami extraction beyond standard practice; this same koji philosophy carries into the umeshu base, giving the fruit liqueur an unusually savoury, textured backbone rather than a flat sugar-and-spirit foundation. The yamahai starter further builds lactic complexity and acidity that cuts through the ume's natural sweetness.

What separates this from the category majority: most commercial umeshu uses a neutral jozo alcohol (distilled spirit) base — this uses a full Junmai sake, meaning the ume fruit macerates against living rice umami rather than neutral spirit. The 1.8 L magnum format (isshobin) is the traditional Japanese hospitality vessel, preserving freshness through reduced surface-area-to-volume ratio.

name: Kozaemon  Shiroku Junmai Kabosu Sake (500 ml)
short_description: Kabosu-infused Junmai from Kozaemon's 1702 Mizunami brewery — umakuchi philosophy meets tart Japanese citrus in a 500 ml format.
description: Founded in 1702 by Nakashima Kozaemon in Mizunami City, Gifu, this brewery has spent over three centuries refining umakuchi — a deliberate, rice-forward richness built on pronounced umami and gentle natural sweetness, a direct counter-position to the light, dry tanrei karakuchi style that dominated Japan's post-1970s market. Shiroku Junmai Kabosu extends that philosophy into flavoured sake territory by incorporating kabosu, a tart, aromatic Japanese citrus native to Oita Prefecture, into a no-added-alcohol Junmai base. The brewery's water source — a natural underground spring fed by snowmelt from Mt. Byobu (Byobuzan) — flows through Mizunami's 70%-forested, cool-humid microclimate, supporting the slow fermentation that builds the textural backbone kabosu acidity then cuts through. Koji is cultivated at approximately 98% humidity to maximise enzyme activity, and the house employs four-stage (yodan) fermentation — a fourth rice-and-water addition beyond the standard three-stage sandan shikomi — extracting deeper umami than conventional brewing achieves. What makes this expression categorically unusual is the pairing of that amplified umami base with kabosu's sharp, yuzu-adjacent tartness: the citrus does not sweeten the sake but instead sharpens its savouriness, functioning more like a seasoning than a flavouring. As a non-vintage blend, it is intended for immediate enjoyment, with the kabosu brightness at its most vivid within 12 months of release.

name: Bijofu  Yuzu (720 ml)
short_description: Yuzu liqueur from Kochi's nansui-water brewery, founded 1904 — coastal Tano-cho citrus meets Bijofu's signature soft-water precision.
description:
Hamakawa Shoten, registered in 1904 in Tano-cho on Kochi's narrow Pacific coastal strip, built its reputation by mastering ultra-soft nansui water drawn from the subterranean Nahari River — a water type once dismissed as unworkable for quality brewing. That same water forms the backbone of this yuzu expression, its near-zero mineral hardness allowing the aromatic oils of yuzu to project without interference from dissolved salts or hardness compounds.

Kochi Prefecture is Japan's foremost yuzu-producing region, and the fruit's characteristic tart, pithy citrus intensity — sharper and more resinous than sudachi or kabosu — is the defining aromatic axis here. The brewery's house yeast tradition (AA-41 and CEL-24, both isoamyl acetate-forward) shapes the fermented base toward melon and soft citrus, creating a layered backdrop rather than a flat spirit-and-juice blend.

What separates this from category peers is the nansui water integration: where most yuzu liqueurs built on harder water read as sharp and one-dimensional, the Nahari River softness rounds the yuzu's pithy edges while preserving its volatile top notes — a textural outcome no technique alone can replicate. NV by nature; drink young to preserve yuzu's volatile aromatic lift.

name: Kinmon Akita Plum Sake Shizuku (300 ml)
Dassai Umeshu (720 ml)
short_description: Dassai's Yamada Nishiki umeshu: the Iwakuni brewery's soft mountain water and centrifuge-pressed sake base meet whole-ume maceration in a single rare expression.
description:
Asahi Shuzo — the Yamaguchi brewery that built its reputation on producing exclusively Junmai Daiginjo and pioneering Japan's first commercial centrifuge press — applies that same obsessive precision to a category it almost never enters. Dassai Umeshu is built on a Junmai Daiginjo sake base rather than the neutral shochu or spirits base used by virtually every other major umeshu producer, anchoring the liqueur in the house's signature soft-water character: the Iwakuni mountain water, classified regionally as Seiryu (clear-stream water) and further purified on-site, yields a low-mineral, texturally silky foundation that carries ume fruit without astringent interference.

The production philosophy mirrors the brewery's sake line: Yamada Nishiki rice, the same 'king of sake rice' used across the entire Dassai range, forms the fermentation base, and the sake is separated from the mash via centrifuge without applied pressure — a technique Dassai commercialised around 2000 — preserving the delicate aromatic integrity that a conventional press would compress away. Whole ume are then macerated into this base, allowing the stone-fruit skin and flesh to contribute both tartaric brightness and a fleshy, almost confited depth. What is categorically unusual here: no other brewery of Dassai's scale commits a Junmai Daiginjo-grade sake base to an umeshu, making this the only expression in the range where the house's centrifuge-pressed, soft-water sake acts as a flavour vehicle rather than the finished product itself.

name: Masumi  Junmai Ginjo karakuchi kiippon (1.8 L)
short_description: Karakuchi 'dry backbone' Junmai Ginjo from Suwa, brewed on Kyokai No. 7 — the yeast Masumi gave the world in 1946.
description: In 1946, Masumi's brewers isolated a yeast strain from their Suwa fermentation tanks that would go on to define modern sake: Kyokai Yeast No. 7, now deployed by over 60% of Japan's breweries. The Karakuchi Kiippon is built entirely on that lineage, fermented in the yamahai yeast-starter method — a two-week natural lactic-acid cultivation in the shubo tank, without commercially added lactic acid — a process Masumi applies across its entire range rather than reserving it for premium tiers alone. The brewery sits at the junction of the ancient Koshu Kaido road and the Kirigamine Highlands approach in Suwa Basin, drawing snowmelt water from the Yatsugatake (Eight Peaks) Range; the cold alpine climate and mineral-clean water drive the pronounced natural acidity that defines the house style. Rice is Miyama Nishiki, grown in Nagano and milled in-house to Junmai Ginjo specification (≤60% remaining grain), then fermented through three-stage sandan-jikomi in temperature-controlled tanks. What sets Kiippon apart within the Masumi range is its deliberate karakuchi (dry) profile — the yamahai lactic backbone amplified rather than softened, producing a sake structured for sustained food pairing rather than standalone sipping. The 1.8 L magnum format preserves freshness through reduced oxygen exposure per volume, making it the format of choice for high-turnover table service.

name: Kirinzan  Junmai Green Bottle (1.8 L)
short_description: Niigata Tanrei Karakuchi in magnum: 100% Aga-machi rice, soft Tokonami River water, cold-fermented with G901 yeast for a razor-dry, umami-threaded finish.
description:
In 1882, third-generation brewer Tokukichi Saito renamed the family operation Kirinzan after the forested mountain rising behind the brewery in Aga-machi — a deliberate act of place-claiming that still defines the house philosophy today. The brewery sits at the foot of Mt. Kirinzan, surrounded by roughly 94% beech and cedar forest; snowmelt and rainfall filter through those forest soils into the Tokonami River basin, yielding exceptionally soft, low-mineral water that is the structural backbone of every Kirinzan expression. Soft water slows saccharification and fermentation, enabling the extended cold fermentation that produces Niigata's signature Tanrei Karakuchi — light-bodied, bone-dry, and clean.

This Junmai is brewed with Gohyaku-mangoku rice milled to ≤70% seimaibuai, fermented with proprietary G901 yeast via the sokujo moto method, and tank-aged in stainless steel before pasteurization — a deliberately unadorned process that refuses to mask the rice and water with aromatic engineering. The 1.8 L magnum format is the traditional isshobin, historically the vessel of choice for izakaya and family tables, and Kirinzan's commitment to treating this everyday format as a serious terroir expression is the brewery's most transgressive stance: nearly 30 years of collaboration with 55 local Aga-machi growers and an in-house agricultural division ensure 100% locally sourced rice even at this tier. The result is a sake whose delicate umami backbone and sharp, clean finish reward both chilled aperitif service and warm kan at 45°C.

name: Kozaemon  Junmai Ginjo Shinano Miyamanishiki (300 ml)
short_description: Miyama Nishiki polished to 55% from Kozaemon's umakuchi-philosophy brewery, brewed with 98%-humidity koji and four-stage yodan fermentation.
description: Founded in 1702 by Nakashima Kozaemon in Mizunami City, Gifu, this brewery made a deliberate commercial declaration in the early 2000s: reject the light, dry tanrei karakuchi mainstream and champion umakuchi — rich, umami-forward sake built for food. That philosophy is not marketing; it is encoded in the production method. Koji is cultivated at approximately 98% humidity, maximising enzyme activity and driving deeper starch conversion than standard cultivation achieves. Fermentation then follows a four-stage yodan addition rather than the industry-standard three-stage sandan shikomi — the fourth rice-and-water addition extending mash complexity and amplifying rice-derived umami extraction. The water source is a natural underground spring fed by snowmelt from Mt. Byobu (Byobuzan), filtered through the forested, cool-humid microclimate of Mizunami's mountainous terrain, lending a clean mineral backbone to the ferment. This expression uses Shinano Miyama Nishiki rice — a cold-climate variety developed in Nagano Prefecture — polished to 55% seimaibuai, qualifying it firmly in the Junmai Ginjo tier. What distinguishes it within the Kozaemon range is the pairing of a delicate ginjo-grade polish with the brewery's characteristically amplified umami technique — a combination most ginjo producers deliberately avoid in favour of aromatic lightness. The result sits at the intersection of floral ginjo lift and the textural richness the house is built on.

name: Kozaemon  Junmai Ginjo Shinano Miyamanishiki (720 ml)
short_description: Miyama Nishiki polished to 55% from Nagano, brewed with 98%-humidity koji and four-stage yodan fermentation for umami depth over dry austerity.
description:
Founded in 1702 by Nakashima Kozaemon in Mizunami City, Gifu, this brewery made a deliberate commercial break from Japan's post-1970s tanrei karakuchi orthodoxy — light, dry sake built for volume — by championing umakuchi: rich, umami-forward brewing designed for food. That philosophy is not marketing; it is encoded in the production method.

Water comes from a natural underground spring fed by snowmelt off Mt. Byobu (Byobuzan), filtered through the forested, cool-humid microclimate of Mizunami's mountainous terrain — conditions that favour slow, low-temperature fermentation and clean mineral character in the base liquid.

The Shinano Miyama Nishiki is brewed with Miyama Nishiki rice polished to 55% seimaibuai (Junmai Ginjo tier), koji cultivated at 98% humidity to maximise enzyme activity, and — critically — four-stage yodan fermentation rather than the standard three-stage sandan shikomi. That fourth addition of rice and water extends mash development, extracting rice-derived umami compounds beyond what conventional ginjo brewing achieves. No distilled alcohol is added.

Miyama Nishiki is a cold-climate Nagano variety rarely used outside its home prefecture, giving this expression a regional specificity absent from Yamada Nishiki-dominant ginjo tiers. Expect a drinking window that rewards near-term consumption while the rice character remains vivid.

name: Kunizakari Peach No Osake (300 ml)
short_description: Peach liqueur from Handa City's 1844 kura — brewed on the Chita Peninsula where Kunizakari also cultivates its own ume fruit in-house.
description: Founded in 1844 by the Nakano family on Aichi's Chita Peninsula — historically one of Japan's foremost brewing hubs thanks to abundant natural spring water and maritime access — Kunizakari built its reputation on bold, food-forward sake before expanding into fruit liqueurs. Peach No Osake sits within that same philosophy: a sake-base fruit liqueur produced at the Handa City brewery, where the house approach to fermentation combines sensor-driven temperature monitoring with toji craftsmanship. The Chita Peninsula's warm, stable climate (annual average ~15.5°C) and natural spring water underpin the base, while peach fruit is layered over the brewery's characteristic full-bodied, umami-rich foundation. Kunizakari operates its own Kuni Zakari Farm for in-house ume cultivation, signalling a vertically integrated approach to fruit sourcing that is rare among traditional kuras. What distinguishes this expression within its category is the deliberate tension between the brewery's robust, savoury house style and the sweetness of peach — a combination engineered for Aichi's bold culinary culture rather than the delicate, aromatic profile typical of Japanese fruit liqueurs. NV by convention; serve well-chilled or over ice.

name: Kunizakari Peach No Osake (720 ml)
short_description: Peach liqueur from Handa City's 1844 Nakano Brewery — fruit-forward, low-tannin, built for bold Aichi table culture.
description: Founded in 1844 by the Nakano family on the Chita Peninsula, Nakano Sake Brewery built its reputation on sake engineered for Aichi's assertive culinary culture — rich miso broths, grilled meats, strong umami. Peach No Osake extends that food-forward philosophy into a fruit liqueur format, drawing on the same abundant natural spring water of the Chita Peninsula that underpins every Kunizakari expression. The brewery also operates Kuni Zakari Farm for in-house fruit cultivation, a rare vertical-integration move that gives the production team direct control over raw material quality — though the specific fruit sourcing for this peach expression is not publicly confirmed. Technically, this is a sake-base fruit liqueur: the nihonshu fermentation platform (koji-driven saccharification, sandan-jikomi moromi) provides a subtly ricey, umami-tinged backbone that distinguishes it from neutral-spirit fruit liqueurs. No wood aging is used; the profile is tank-fresh, preserving primary peach aromatics without oak interference. What sets this apart in its category is that underlying sake structure — a faint lactic grain note beneath the fruit — that no Western peach schnapps or crème de pêche can replicate. Serve well-chilled or over ice; as a NV expression, there is no meaningful cellaring upside.

Kirinzan Shooting Star (720 ml)
short_description: Niigata Tanrei Karakuchi from Aga-machi's beech-filtered snowmelt — 100% locally grown Gohyakumangoku, cold-fermented with G901 yeast for razor-dry precision.
description: In 1882, third-generation brewer Tokukichi Saito renamed the family operation — founded in 1843 at the foot of Mt. Kirinzan — after the mountain itself, anchoring the brewery's identity to a single place. That commitment to place became literal: after nearly 30 years of collaboration with 55 local Aga-machi farmers, Kirinzan achieved 100% locally sourced rice, supported today by an in-house agricultural division established in 2011. Shooting Star draws on Gohyakumangoku rice, Niigata's signature variety, polished to 55% (seimaibuai), grown within 10 km of the brewery in a valley where 94% of the surrounding land is dense beech and cedar forest. The brewery's water — drawn from the Tokonami River basin and naturally filtered through those forest soils — is characteristically soft and low-mineral, the physical foundation of Niigata's Tanrei Karakuchi (light and dry) style. Fermentation proceeds via sokujo moto starter using proprietary G901 yeast, a Niigata-associated strain that drives extended cold fermentation without generating the high-ester aromatic profile typical of ginjo-focused breweries. The result is a Junmai Ginjo that deliberately refuses aromatic showmanship: delicate umami backbone, a clean mid-palate, and a finish that cuts sharply rather than lingers sweetly. Tank-aged in stainless steel and pasteurized before bottling at 15–16% ABV, it is not genshu. The special feature here is philosophical: Kirinzan treats this accessible tier with the same craft rigour as its Junmai Daiginjo, making terroir-driven restraint the point rather than a compromise.

name: Kirinzan Extra Dry (1.8 L)
short_description: Niigata Tanrei Karakuchi in magnum — soft Tokonami River water, G901 yeast, Gohyakumangoku rice milled to 65%, brewed at the foot of Mt. Kirinzan since 1843.
description:
In 1882, third-generation Tokukichi Saito renamed the family brewery after the mountain that shadows it — Mt. Kirinzan — anchoring the brand's identity to a single forested site in Aga-machi, Higashi-kanbara-gun, where approximately 94% of the surrounding landscape is dense beech and cedar forest. That forest is not incidental: it filters the soft, low-mineral water drawn from the Tokonami River basin, the hydrological backbone of Kirinzan's signature style. Soft water slows saccharification and fermentation, enabling the extended cold fermentation that defines Niigata's Tanrei Karakuchi — light, dry, and structurally precise rather than aromatic or rich.

This Extra Dry expression is built on a Gohyakumangoku and Koshi-ibuki blend milled to 65% seimaibuai, fermented with proprietary G901 yeast via the sokujo (quick-starter) moto method, then tank-aged in stainless steel and pasteurized before bottling at standard ABV. The 1.8 L magnum format — the traditional isshobin — is the vessel in which izakayas and Japanese households have always consumed everyday sake, and Kirinzan treats this futsushu tier with the same sourcing discipline applied to its Junmai Daiginjo: 100% locally grown rice from 55 Aga-machi farming families, a supply chain that took nearly 30 years to build. The special feature here is precisely that seriousness of intent applied to an entry-level format — terroir-driven futsushu is a category almost no prestige brewery bothers to defend.

name: Kirinzan Classic (720 ml)
short_description: Niigata Tanrei Karakuchi from Mt. Kirinzan's foot — Gohyakumangoku/Koshi-ibuki milled to 65%, soft Tokonami River water, G901 yeast.
description: When the Saito family pivoted from charcoal manufacturing to sake brewing in 1843, they chose a site at the foot of Mt. Kirinzan in Aga-machi — a decision that locked in the brewery's defining raw material: exceptionally soft, low-mineral water drawn from the Tokonami River basin, filtered through dense beech forest soils. That water chemistry is not incidental; it slows fermentation, suppresses harsh acids, and produces the clean, dry finish that defines Niigata's Tanrei Karakuchi school. Kirinzan Classic is built on a blend of Gohyakumangoku and Koshi-ibuki rice, milled to 65% seimaibuai, fermented with the brewery's proprietary G901 yeast via the sokujo moto starter method and extended cold fermentation. Tank-aged in stainless steel and pasteurized before bottling at 15–16% ABV, it is diluted to standard strength — no genshu concentration. The transgression here is deliberate: Kirinzan treats this entry-level futsushu as a serious craft expression of local terroir, sourcing all rice from within 10 km of the brewery through 55 contracted Aga-machi growers — a 30-year supply-chain project that most prestige breweries reserve only for their flagship tiers. The result is a razor-sharp, lightly umami-framed dry sake that performs across a wide temperature range.

name: Kirinzan Classic (1.8 L)
short_description: Niigata Tanrei Karakuchi in a 1.8 L format — Gohyakumangoku + Koshi-ibuki milled to 65%, brewed with soft Tokonami River water since 1843.
description: When the Saito family pivoted from charcoal manufacturing to sake brewing in 1843, they planted their operation at the foot of Mt. Kirinzan in Aga-machi — a decision that locked them into one of Japan's most demanding brewing environments and, ultimately, one of its most distinctive house styles. The brewery draws soft, low-mineral water from the Tokonami River basin, naturally filtered through dense beech and cedar forest soils, a water profile that enables the extended, slow cold fermentation that defines Niigata's Tanrei Karakuchi (light and dry) philosophy. Kirinzan Classic is built on a blend of Gohyakumangoku and Koshi-ibuki rice, milled to 65% seimaibuai, fermented with the brewery's proprietary G901 yeast strain via sokujo moto starter, then tank-aged in stainless steel before pasteurization. What separates this expression from commodity futsushu is institutional conviction: Kirinzan spent nearly 30 years building a network of 55 local Aga-machi growers to achieve 100% locally sourced rice — a supply chain now managed by an in-house agricultural division — and applies that same sourcing discipline to its entry-level Classic as to its Junmai Daiginjo. The 1.8 L format delivers the brewery's razor-sharp, delicate umami backbone at everyday scale without compromise. Drink now; serve chilled, at room temperature, or warmed to explore the full range of the soft-water fermentation character.

name: Kirinzan Dento Karakuchi Genshu (300 ml)
short_description: Undiluted genshu from Aga-machi's Tokonami basin — Kirinzan's Tanrei Karakuchi philosophy at full, uncut 18–19% ABV intensity.
description:
When the Saito family renamed their Aga-machi brewery Kirinzan in 1882, they were staking an identity on the mountain at their doorstep — and on the soft, low-mineral water filtering through beech-forest soils into the Tokonami River basin below it. That water is the technical foundation of everything: its near-zero mineral load enables the extended, slow cold fermentation that defines Niigata's Tanrei Karakuchi (light-and-dry) school.

The Dento Karakuchi Genshu is bottled undiluted — genshu — a deliberate departure from Kirinzan's standard practice of diluting to 15–16% ABV. The rice, drawn from within a 10 km radius of the brewery (the result of nearly 30 years of collaboration with 55 local Aga-machi growers), is milled and fermented using proprietary yeast G901 via the sokujo moto method. Stainless-steel tank aging preserves the clean, unadorned grain character the brewery prizes above aromatic showmanship.

What separates this expression is the genshu format applied to a dry, classically structured Honjozo-style profile — most undiluted sake leans rich or sweet; here the higher ABV sharpens the already razor-edged finish rather than softening it. Drink now through two years, slightly chilled or at room temperature to let the undiluted weight open fully.

name: Koimari Saki Zenryo Oyamanishiki Junmai Ginjo (720 ml)
Masumi Junmai Ginjo Kuro Black (300 ml)
short_description: Suwa-brewed Junmai Ginjo from the birthplace of Kyokai Yeast No. 7 — yamahai method, Miyama Nishiki rice, alpine snowmelt water.
description: In 1946, Masumi's brewers isolated a yeast strain from their own fermentation tanks in Suwa, Nagano — a discovery that became Kyokai Yeast No. 7, now deployed by over 60% of Japan's sake breweries. That same proprietary yeast drives every bottle in the range, including this Junmai Ginjo. The brewery sits at the junction of the ancient Koshu Kaido road and the Kirigamine Highlands approach, in a district long called Shimizu-machi — 'Clear Water Village' — where water drawn from the Yatsugatake (Eight Peaks) Range snowmelt delivers exceptional softness and mineral clarity to the fermentation. Primary rice is Miyama Nishiki, a Nagano-bred cold-climate variety polished to Ginjo grade (≤60% remaining), milled in-house at the brewery's own polishing plant. Fermentation proceeds via the yamahai yeast-starter method: natural lactic-acid bacteria are cultivated over approximately two weeks in the shubo tank before yeast is introduced — no commercially added lactic acid — yielding higher natural acidity and deeper lactic complexity than the modern sokujo norm. What sets Masumi apart categorically is the house-wide application of yamahai across the entire range, not merely reserved for prestige tiers, making this 300 ml format a rare entry point into a genuinely labour-intensive tradition. The result is structured, umami-forward, and built for the table — the founding Miyasaka philosophy of sake so balanced it disappears with the meal.

name: Miinokotobuki Junmai Ginjo Yamada-Nishiki Hougin (720 ml)
Miinokotobuki Yamahai Junmai Shinjo Biden (720 ml)
Sakunohana Kumamoto Kobo Junmai (720 ml)
short_description: Kumamoto kobo junmai from Nagano's 700 m Saku-daira plateau — slow cold fermentation, Hitogokochi rice, no added alcohol.
description:
Sakunohana Shuzo has brewed in Saku City since 1892, operating from a plateau at roughly 700 m elevation between Mt. Asama and the Yatsugatake range — a high-altitude inland basin where cold winters and clean snowmelt-fed groundwater define the character of every fermentation. Saku-daira's sharp diurnal swings slow microbial activity, naturally tightening texture and preserving aromatic precision in the finished sake.

This Junmai is built entirely on Hitogokochi rice grown in Nagano Prefecture, polished to the Junmai threshold with no added distilled alcohol — only rice and rice koji. Fermentation follows the ginjo-zukuri method under toji Tatsuyuki Inoue: slow, low-temperature fermentation that coaxes restrained fruit character without sacrificing the dry, clean finish the brewery is known for. The Kumamoto kobo (yeast) designation signals use of the celebrated No. 9 yeast lineage, prized for its stable, fruit-forward fermentation profile at low temperatures.

What sets this expression apart within Sakunohana's lineup is the explicit Kumamoto kobo declaration on a Junmai-grade bottling — a yeast choice more commonly reserved for Ginjo and Daiginjo tiers — delivering ginjo-adjacent aromatics at a Junmai price point. Drink now through two to three years; serve at 10–15 °C to keep the fruit lifted and the dry finish clean.

name: Sakunohana Kumamoto Kobo Junmai (1.8 L)
short_description: Hitogokochi Junmai from 700 m Saku-daira plateau — Kumamoto yeast, slow cold fermentation, 1.8 L format for the table.
description:
Founded in 1892 in Saku City, Sakunohana Shuzo sits at roughly 700 m elevation on the Saku-daira plateau, a high-altitude inland basin flanked by Mt. Asama to the north and the Yatsugatake range to the south — a geography that delivers the sustained cold temperatures essential to slow, controlled fermentation. The brewery draws on the regional snowmelt-fed groundwater characteristic of the Chikuma River corridor, where local rice paddies have supplied sake brewers since the Edo period.

This Junmai is built entirely from Hitogokochi rice — Nagano's own sake variety — with no added distilled alcohol, expressing the grain without dilution or correction. The designation Kumamoto Kobo identifies the yeast: Kumamoto yeast (Kyokai No. 9), originally developed at the Kumamoto Prefectural Brewery and prized for producing clean, fruity ginjo-type aromatics even in Junmai-grade fermentations — an unusual choice that lifts the aromatic register well above what the polish ratio alone would suggest. Toji Tatsuyuki Inoue applies ginjo-zukuri slow low-temperature fermentation as standard practice across the lineup.

The 1.8 L magnum format makes this the most economical entry into Sakunohana's terroir-driven house style. The Saku-daira cool climate imparts a dry, clean finish with lively acidity that keeps the medium body from feeling heavy.

name: Código 1530 Tequila Origen Extra Anejo (750 ml)
short_description: 6+ years in uncharred French White Oak ex-Napa Cabernet barrels — Código 1530's Lowlands agave aged longer than any CRT minimum, zero additives.
description:
When Ron Snyder, Federico Vaughan, and George Strait commercialised a multi-generational private recipe from Amatitán in 2016, their most transgressive decision was structural: every aged expression would mature exclusively in uncharred French White Oak ex-Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon barrels — not the ex-bourbon American oak that defines virtually every other Jalisco house. Origen Extra Añejo is where that decision reaches its fullest expression, spending six or more years in those wine-seasoned casks.

The agave grows in the Los Bajos (Lowlands) volcanic soils of Amatitán, where rain-fed fertility and lower elevation produce piñas harvested only at ≥32 Brix — a sugar threshold the distillery enforces manually at intake. Process water is filtered through the same volcanic rock that shapes the soil, threading a mineral thread through every stage.

Fermentation is driven by heirloom bread yeast from a local Amatitán bakery, running four to twelve days in stainless steel — far slower than industry-standard accelerated fermentation — building earthy, mineral complexity before double distillation in custom handmade stainless steel pot stills. No glycerin, colorants, or sweeteners are added at any stage.

The special feature: French White Oak's eugenol-rich stave chemistry delivers clove spice and red-fruit lift where American oak would give coconut-vanilla — after six-plus years, that divergence from category convention is profound and unmistakable.

As a non-vintage spirit, Origen drinks in a mature, contemplative register now; the extended wood contact rewards slow sipping at room temperature.

The Glenlivet Collection @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description: Minmore Farm, Livet Valley: lantern-still geometry and wooden washbacks define Speyside's founding fruit-forward house style since 1824.
description: In 1824, George Smith became the first distiller in the Scottish Highlands to take out a legal licence under the Excise Act — a deliberate act of transgression against the illicit trade — and built a house style so influential that dozens of competitors hyphenated '-Glenlivet' onto their own names until a landmark 1884 trademark ruling gave the Smith family sole right to the name. That founding philosophy was technical: make a lighter, cleaner, fruit-forward spirit by design, not by accident. The Minmore Farm site sits high above the River Livet, fed by Josie's Well, a natural mountain spring descending from the surrounding hills — cold, mineral-lean water that contributes to the distillery's characteristically clean wort. Fermentation runs approximately 48 hours in wooden washbacks, retained at higher maintenance cost specifically for the floral, estery quality wood imparts over stainless steel. Double distillation through 14 lantern-shaped copper pot stills — pinched waist, long narrow neck — maximises copper reflux; the narrow heart cut of roughly 18–20% of total distillate strips impurities and locks in purity. Core expressions are chill-filtered, 40% ABV, with caramel colouring, a bottling philosophy that prioritises consistency and accessibility over raw cask character. The special feature here is historical weight: no other Speyside name carries the legal and stylistic authority of 'The Glenlivet' as a standalone designation. Approachable now, the fruit-and-vanilla core holds well over 3–5 years in bottle.

name: Royal Salute @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description: Blended at Strathisla's dedicated Vault, every component — malt and grain alike — carries a minimum 21-year age statement, a standard unique across any Scotch portfolio.
description: Created on 2 June 1953 to mark the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II, Royal Salute was built around a single non-negotiable constraint: no component whisky — malt or grain — may enter the blend with fewer than 21 years in oak, a floor that remains absolute across every expression in the range and that no other Scotch portfolio matches at its entry tier. The brand home is Strathisla Distillery, Keith, Moray — founded 1786 and the oldest continuously operating distillery in the Scottish Highlands — where process water is drawn from the Fons Bulliens Well, a peat-free natural spring prized for its softness; that mineral-clean water profile underpins the blend's characteristically smooth, fruit-forward texture. Malt components are double-distilled through short, squat pot stills (wash stills 12,500 L; spirit stills 8,500 L), a geometry that retains body and orchard-fruit character, then married with grain whiskies from partner distilleries across all five Scottish regions. Maturation spans ex-bourbon American oak and ex-sherry casks, held in the dedicated Royal Salute Vault within Strathisla's bonded warehouses, where Speyside's mild, humid valley microclimate promotes slow, even spirit development over the full two-decade-plus rest. The Signature Blend is bottled at 40% ABV and presented in a hand-crafted porcelain flagon — produced from Cornish clay with double-layer glazing over up to six days — that functions as a secondary age-worthy object in its own right. As a non-vintage blend, character is consistent across releases: ripe pear, dried citrus peel, vanilla cream, and a gently spiced oak finish shaped by the long maturation floor rather than any single harvest.

name: The Macallan Sherry Oak @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description: Easter Elchies Estate, Speyside: 100% Oloroso-seasoned European oak, no caramel, squat spirit stills at 3,900L — the original sherry-bomb architecture.
description: Alexander Reid founded the Easter Elchies distillery in 1824 on the north-west slopes of the River Spey valley — and the decision to build curiously small, squat spirit stills at just 3,900 litres (craft-distillery scale, despite global output) was deliberate: limited copper-vapour contact produces a heavier, oilier new make engineered to absorb decades of sherry-cask influence rather than express grain character. That philosophy is more aggressive than any Speyside peer. The 485-acre Easter Elchies Estate sits on alluvial soils above the Spey, with on-site spring water used for mashing — fertile ground that yields the barley, but it is the cask, not the terroir, that drives flavour here. Sherry Oak is matured exclusively in 100% ex-Oloroso sherry-seasoned European oak, sourced from Galicia, Asturias, and Cantabria, air-dried for approximately one year, coopered in Spain, and seasoned for 18 months in Jerez before a single fill — a vertically integrated wood programme unmatched in Speyside. No caramel colouring (E150a) is added; every shade of amber is earned from the cask alone. What separates Sherry Oak from the Double Cask is absolute: zero American oak influence, making this the purest expression of the Oloroso-driven house signature — dried fig, dark raisin, stem ginger, and bitter chocolate without the vanilla softening of American wood. Bottled at 43% ABV; standard expression is chill-filtered.

name: Glenmorangie Grand Vintage @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description: Tain's limestone-filtered Tarlogie spring water meets Scotland's tallest stills (5.14 m) in a rare Grand Vintage release from the Dornoch Firth coast.
description:
When William Matheson converted Morangie Farm's brewery into a distillery in 1843, he acquired second-hand London gin stills — their extraordinary height (5.14 m, still the tallest in Scotland) was an accident of origin that became Glenmorangie's defining technical signature. That copper column enforces extreme reflux, stripping out heavy congeners and producing one of the lightest, most floral new-make spirits in the Highlands — the structural opposite of short-still houses like Macallan.

The distillery sits on the southern shore of the Dornoch Firth in Tain, Ross-shire, drawing water from Tarlogie Springs: rainwater that filters through limestone and sandstone for up to 100 years before emerging hard and mineral-rich — atypical for Scotland and a direct contributor to the spirit's clean, precise character. Glenmorangie purchased ~650 acres of the Tarlogie catchment in the 1980s solely to protect this source.

Spirit is distilled from very lightly peated malt through a tight heart cut (74% down to 60% ABV), then matured in American white oak ex-bourbon casks sourced from Glenmorangie's own Ozark Mountain forests in Missouri — staves air-dried two years, bourbon-seasoned four years before a drop of spirit enters. The Grand Vintage designation marks a departure from the standard non-age-statement core range, selecting specific distillation years for individual release.

What sets this expression apart is the combination of vintage-dated provenance and the distillery's proprietary full-chain wood management — a level of cask supply control virtually unmatched in Scotch production. Drink now through the next decade; the Tarlogie minerality and orchard-fruit character from the tall stills will continue to integrate with extended cellaring.

name: Jameson Collection @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description: Single-distillery blend from Midleton, Cork — triple-distilled over unpeated gas-kilned malt, aged min. 4 years in ex-bourbon and ex-sherry casks.
description: John Jameson's decision to include unmalted barley alongside malted barley in his pot still mash was originally a workaround for 18th-century Irish malt taxes — yet that fiscal pragmatism became the defining signature of Single Pot Still Irish Whiskey, a style that exists nowhere else on earth. Production has operated from New Midleton Distillery, County Cork since 1975, drawing water from the Dungourney River and barley from over 200 farms within 50 miles of the site. Crucially, barley is dried in a closed gas-fired kiln — not peat — preserving a clean, unsmoked malt character that separates Jameson categorically from most Scotch blends. The house style marries Single Pot Still whiskey (malted and unmalted barley, triple-distilled in copper pot stills) with maize-based grain whiskey (triple-distilled in column stills), matured a minimum of 4 years in a combination of ex-bourbon American oak and ex-sherry European oak butts. The special feature here is structural: unlike virtually every other major blended whiskey house, Jameson sources all component whiskeys from a single distillery — Midleton — giving the blending team grain-to-glass control over consistency that multi-source blends cannot replicate. The result is a whiskey whose approachability is an engineered outcome, not an accident.

name: Chivas Regal Collection @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description: Strathisla-anchored blended Scotch from the 1786 Keith distillery — short-necked pot stills, granite-filtered spring water, Speyside malt-forward house code.
description: When Charles Stewart Howard launched Chivas Regal in 1909 as a 25-year-old blend, no competitor carried such an age statement — a deliberate act that invented the premium blended Scotch category and set the malt-heavy philosophy still governing every expression today. The heart malt is Strathisla, drawn from the oldest continuously operating distillery in the Scottish Highlands (founded 1786, Keith, Moray), where process water travels one mile from the Broomhill Spring, naturally filtered through granite, lending a soft mineral backbone to the new make. Short-necked wash stills at Strathisla allow heavier vapours to rise unchecked, producing a full-bodied, generous spirit that resists the lean, grain-forward profile of most volume blends. Fermentation runs in traditional wooden washbacks — not stainless steel — providing thermal insulation for a steady, flavour-building two-day ferment. Component whiskies rest in American ex-bourbon and European ex-Oloroso sherry oak, with the age statement on any bottle marking the youngest whisky present; older Longmorn, Miltonduff, Braeval, and Strathclyde grain components contribute without declaration. The Speyside-centric malt-over-peat philosophy — deliberately rounder than Islay-influenced or grain-heavy peers — remains the defining house transgression in a category that often chases smoke or grain efficiency over orchard-fruit richness.

name: Nikka Taketsuru @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description: Blended malt from Yoichi's coal-fired stills and Miyagikyo's steam-heated pot stills — Taketsuru's two-distillery palette in a single bottle.
description: When Masataka Taketsuru founded Yoichi in 1934 and later Miyagikyo in 1969, he did so with deliberate intent: Japanese regulations barring distillate exchange between competing groups forced him to engineer his own full blending spectrum in-house — two distilleries built as stylistic opposites so that no outside malt would ever be needed. Taketsuru Pure Malt is the direct expression of that philosophy, a blended malt drawing exclusively from both sites with no grain whisky in the recipe. Yoichi, on Hokkaido's northwest coast at the base of the Shakotan Peninsula, uses direct coal-fired pot stills with downward lyne arms — one of the last distilleries in the world to retain this labour-intensive method — producing heavier congeners and a maritime, coastal character shaped by Sea of Japan sea-breeze and river fog. Miyagikyo, set in a forested valley fed by the Hirose and Nikkawa rivers drawing from the Zao Mountain Range, runs steam-heated pot stills with bulging necks and upward lyne arms, maximising reflux for a lighter, fruit-forward spirit from water among the softest of any Japanese distillery. The marriage of these two structurally opposed malts — heavy and peated against floral and delicate — is what no single-site Japanese producer can replicate. Bottled at standard strength, this non-vintage expression delivers consistent house character across releases.

name: Kingdom of Cognac @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
Yamazaki Tsukuriwake Collection @Blue Moon Siam Paragon
short_description: Yamazaki's radical self-deconstruction: each Tsukuriwake bottling isolates one production variable — cask, grain, or peat — at 48% ABV from Japan's oldest malt distillery.
description:
When 5th-generation Chief Blender Shinji Fukuyo expanded the Tsukuriwake concept in 2024 beyond cask type to include raw ingredient selection and peat provenance, he made explicit what most distillers keep invisible: that Yamazaki's house style is not a single recipe but a deliberate convergence of independently managed variables. Tsukuriwake — 'artisanship through a diversity of making' — is the philosophical engine behind this series.

The distillery sits in a misty valley at the confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers in Shimamoto-cho, Osaka Prefecture, where tea master Sen no Rikyu once built a teahouse to exploit the same exceptionally soft water. Japan's four distinct seasons drive an angel's share of up to 5% annually in uncontrolled dunnage warehouses — roughly double the Scottish rate — compressing wood-spirit integration into a tighter timeline.

Each expression in the collection is vatted within a single cask category: American oak puncheons (480-litre, virgin or first-fill, coopered in-house), Spanish oak ex-Oloroso sherry butts, Hokkaido Mizunara (Quercus mongolica) butts at a minimum 18 years, or peated malt variants sourced from Islay or Japanese peat. All are bottled at 48% ABV — above the standard Yamazaki core range — to preserve aromatic intensity without reaching cask strength.

The structural transgression here is categorical: because Japanese distillers do not trade casks with competitors, Yamazaki must manufacture every flavour dimension internally, and the Tsukuriwake series releases each dimension as a standalone bottling — a transparency no peer in the Japanese whisky category has replicated at this scale.

Johnnie Walker  Red Label (700 ml)
short_description: 30+ Scotch whiskies blended across Speyside, Islay, Highland & Lowland — John Walker's tea-merchant logic applied to whisky at global scale.
description: When John Walker opened his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, he applied the same cross-origin blending logic he used for tea — the insight that combining sources eliminates seasonal variation — to Scotch whisky, effectively inventing the modern concept of consistent, branded blended whisky. Red Label is the entry point to that architecture: over 30 malt and grain whiskies drawn from all four major Scottish regions, with east-coast Speyside malts (honeyed, fruit-forward) counterweighted against smokier west-coast and Islay contributors. Cardhu (Speyside) anchors the blend's sweetness; Caol Ila (Islay) delivers clean maritime peat; the grain whisky backbone — produced via continuous column (Coffey) still distillation from wheat or maize — provides the blending canvas. Maturation is in ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks, with no age statement declared; the blend is chill-filtered and bottled at 40% ABV. What distinguishes Red Label within the blended Scotch category is sheer compositional breadth: no single-region producer can thread smoke, sweetness, and grain spice simultaneously across 30+ components the way a pan-Scotland blending house can. Master blender Dr. Emma Walker — the first woman to hold the role in the brand's 200-year history — draws from a reserve of approximately 10 million casks to maintain batch-to-batch consistency at commercial scale, a technical feat as demanding as any single-malt program.

name: Johnnie Walker  Red Label (Mini-Bar 200 ml)
short_description: 30+ Scotch whiskies blended across Speyside, Islay & Highland — John Walker's tea-trade insight bottled at minibar scale.
description: When John Walker opened his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, his competitive edge was borrowed from the tea trade: blend across origins and seasons to eliminate batch variation — a principle that made Scotch whisky commercially scalable for the first time. Red Label is the direct descendant of that logic, drawing on over 30 malt and grain whiskies sourced from all four major Scottish regions. The architecture is deliberate: rich east-coast Speyside malts (Cardhu's honeyed sweetness anchors the core) are threaded against smoky west-coast and island contributors, with Caol Ila's clean maritime peat providing the lift that prevents the blend from reading as merely grain-sweet. Maturation relies on ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks, with a legal minimum of three years; Red Label carries no age statement, allowing the blending team — currently led by Dr. Emma Walker, the first woman to hold the master blender role in the brand's 200-year history — to select for flavour profile rather than age. The expression is chill-filtered and bottled at 40% ABV. What distinguishes Red Label within the blended Scotch category is sheer breadth of source material: no single-region producer can replicate the simultaneous presence of Speyside fruit, Islay smoke, and Highland wax in one glass. This 200 ml format delivers the full house character without commitment to a full bottle.

name: Johnnie Walker  Black Label (Mini-Bar 200 ml)
short_description: ~40 malt & grain whiskies, all 12+ years in ex-bourbon & ex-Sherry oak — Cardhu sweetness, Caol Ila peat, Clynelish wax in 200 ml.
description: When John Walker opened his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, his tea-blending instinct — that combining sources eliminates batch variation — became the founding logic of the world's most consistent blended Scotch. Black Label is where that logic reaches its most deliberate expression. Approximately 40 malt and grain whiskies, every component aged a minimum of 12 years in predominantly ex-bourbon American oak and ex-Sherry European oak casks, are assembled by master blender Dr. Emma Walker — the first woman to hold the role in the brand's 200-year history — from a reserve of roughly 10 million casks. The architecture is intentionally pan-Scotland: Cardhu (Speyside) anchors the blend with honeyed fruit; Caol Ila (Islay) threads in clean maritime peat smoke; Clynelish (Highland) contributes a distinctive waxy coastal depth. No single-region producer can replicate this triangulation. The 200 ml format delivers the identical 40% ABV, chill-filtered blend as the full bottle — the same batch-to-batch consistency that made Black Label a global benchmark, now in a format built for exploration or travel. Non-vintage by design; drink immediately or hold indefinitely.

name: Johnnie Walker & Son  King George V (750 ml)
short_description: Royal warrant-bearing blend of rare closed-distillery whiskies, hand-selected from 10 million casks — Cardhu, Caol Ila, Clynelish architecture at its most ceremonial.
description:
When King George V granted Johnnie Walker a Royal Warrant in 1934, it codified what the blending house had already proven: that pan-Scotland assemblage, not single-distillery provenance, was the most demanding discipline in Scotch. The King George V expression is the direct heir to that warrant — a blend constructed specifically to honour that royal recognition, drawing from distilleries that were operational during the monarch's reign, including whiskies from permanently closed 'ghost' distilleries no longer capable of producing a single new cask.

The blend's architecture follows the house's three-pillar structure: Cardhu (Speyside) delivers honeyed, orchard-fruit sweetness as the structural core; Caol Ila (Islay) introduces clean maritime peat smoke; and Clynelish (Highland) contributes its signature waxy, coastal depth — a combination achievable only by a blending house with access to approximately 10 million casks across all major Scottish regions. Selection is governed by individual cask analysis, with each barrel approved by the blending team before entry into the final assemblage.

What separates King George V from the standard luxury tier is the deliberate inclusion of whiskies from distilleries that no longer exist — a historically irreplaceable component that cannot be replicated in future batches. Bottled at 43% ABV, presented in a decanter-format bottle with a ceramic stopper, this is a non-vintage, non-age-statement release whose rarity is defined by cask availability, not calendar year.

name: Glenfarclas  Heritage Speyside Single Malt Scotch Whisky (700 ml)
short_description: Direct gas-fired Speyside malt from Ben Rinnes, sixth-generation Grant family; Oloroso sherry butt maturation, natural colour, no chill-filtration.
description:
When the Grant family reverted from steam heating back to direct gas-fired copper pot stills in the early 1980s — abandoning the change within weeks after finding steam fundamentally stripped the spirit's depth — they codified what Glenfarclas has always been: a distillery that refuses to compromise texture for convenience. That decision still shapes every drop produced at Rechlerich Farm, Ballindalloch, on the north-western slopes of Ben Rinnes at roughly 244 m elevation, where soft, slightly mineral spring water from Ben Rinnes feeds the mash tun and the surrounding Banffshire climate provides slow, even maturation in traditional dunnage warehouses with earth floors and stone walls.

The Heritage expression draws on Glenfarclas's defining cask regime: Spanish Oloroso sherry butts sourced exclusively from José Miguel Martín, reused across multiple fills to calibrate wood influence without overwhelming the spirit. The six large pot stills — wash stills of approximately 29,600 L each, all direct-fired — generate a characteristically oily, Maillard-rich new make that integrates the dried-fruit and roasted-nut register of Oloroso oak over time. Natural colour and no caramel addition are non-negotiable house standards across the range.

What sets Heritage apart within the Glenfarclas lineup is its entry-point accessibility without sacrificing the house's sherry-dominant architecture — a rare proposition in a portfolio where the distillery otherwise insists on full age statements from 10 to 40 years. Expect dark dried fruit, fruitcake spice, roasted hazelnuts, and a faint Ben Rinnes mineral thread on the finish.
