name: Suntory  Yamazaki 18 years old Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description: Japan's first malt distillery, 1923 — Yamazaki 18 draws significantly on rare Mizunara oak, imparting sandalwood and incense no other wood delivers.
description:
When Shinjirō Torii broke ground at the misty foot of Mount Tennōzan in 1923, he chose a site that tea master Sen no Rikyū had already declared sacred for its water — soft aquifer water fed by the confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers, drawn at roughly 25 metres elevation in one of Japan's most humid microclimates. That warmth and moisture accelerate maturation in ways no highland distillery can replicate.

Yamazaki's Tsukuriwake philosophy drives the 18-year-old's complexity: fermentation runs 64–80 hours across both Oregon pine wooden washbacks and stainless-steel tanks using multiple yeast strains, while distillation cycles through pot stills of deliberately varied shapes — squat, tall, ball-necked — with both worm-tub and shell-and-tube condensers to generate a wide palette of malt styles from a single site. At 18 years, the cask regime shifts meaningfully toward Japanese Mizunara oak (Quercus mongolica), alongside ex-bourbon American white oak and Oloroso sherry casks — a proportion that distinguishes it sharply from the 12-year expression.

Mizunara is the category transgression: so porous it is nearly impossible to cooperate, used almost nowhere else in world whisky, yet it alone delivers the sandalwood, kyara incense, and agarwood character that defines Yamazaki's upper tier. Bottled at 43% ABV with chill-filtration in the standard release, the 18-year-old sits at the threshold where Mizunara influence becomes unmistakable rather than merely present.

name: The Macallan  Rare Cask (700 ml)
short_description: Easter Elchies Estate: Rare Cask draws from over 4,000 hand-selected sherry-seasoned casks annually, bottled at natural colour with no caramel addition.
description:
The Macallan's cask programme is vertically integrated to a degree unmatched in Speyside — the distillery controls oak sourcing from Galician and Asturian forests, air-drying staves for approximately one year, coopering, and an 18-month Oloroso sherry-seasoning in Jerez before a single litre of spirit is filled. Rare Cask is the expression that pushes this philosophy furthest: master whisky maker and a small panel select fewer than 4,000 individual casks per year from the Easter Elchies Estate warehouses — 16 dunnage and 35 racked — drawing exclusively from sherry-seasoned European and American oak to build a vatting of exceptional depth and chromatic intensity.

Easter Elchies sits on the north-west slopes of the River Spey valley near Craigellachie, Moray, where alluvial soils and on-site spring water feed the mash. The distillery's 24 spirit stills at just 3,900 litres each — craft-distillery scale despite global production — are deliberately squat and small, replicating historic Macallan shapes cast from 'death masks' of the originals by coppersmith Forsyths. This geometry limits copper-vapour contact and produces a heavier, oilier new make capable of absorbing decades of sherry-cask influence without structural collapse. No caramel colouring (E150a) is added; the deep mahogany hue is entirely cask-derived. What distinguishes Rare Cask within the portfolio is its cross-vintage, cross-cask-type assembly — a moving target rebuilt each year from a different constellation of exceptional wood — making no two annual releases identical in character.

name: The Macallan  25 Year Old Sherry Oak (700 ml)
short_description: 25 years in 100% Oloroso-seasoned European oak from Galicia — Macallan's upstream cask control, from forest to filled barrel, makes this the category's most wood-integrated expression.
description: Alexander Reid founded the Easter Elchies distillery in 1824, but the decision that defines this whisky was made long before filling: Macallan controls its oak programme from Galician and Cantabrian forests through air-drying, coopering, and 18-month Oloroso seasoning in Jerez — a vertical integration unmatched by any Speyside peer. The Easter Elchies Estate sits on the north-west slopes of the River Spey valley near Craigellachie, where alluvial soils and on-site spring water feed a mash tun reportedly the largest in Scotland. The 25 Year Old Sherry Oak matures exclusively in 100% ex-Oloroso sherry-seasoned European oak casks — no American oak, no blending across wood types — producing a concentration of dried fruit, dark chocolate, and warm spice that the Double Cask range structurally cannot replicate. That depth is engineered upstream: Macallan's spirit stills run at just 3,900 litres — craft-distillery scale — deliberately squat and small to limit copper-vapour contact, yielding a heavier, oilier new make capable of absorbing 25 years of sherry-wood extraction without losing structural integrity. Natural colour only; no E150a caramel added. The special feature here is the combination of quarter-century maturation with single-wood-type discipline: every molecule of colour and flavour derives from one cask type, one seasoning regime, one forest origin — a purity of wood expression that longer-aged blended-cask releases sacrifice for complexity.

name: Johnnie Walker  Aged 18 Years (750 ml)
short_description: Pan-Scotland blend architecture — Cardhu sweetness, Caol Ila smoke, Clynelish wax — minimum 18 years in ex-bourbon and ex-Sherry oak.
description: When John Walker opened his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, his tea-blending instinct — that combining sources across regions eliminates batch variation — became the founding logic of the world's most architecturally deliberate blended Scotch. The Aged 18 Years applies that same logic at extended maturation: every component whisky, drawn from Diageo's network spanning Speyside, Highland, Islay, and Lowland, must reach a minimum of 18 years in oak before the blending team considers it. The cask regime combines ex-bourbon American oak barrels and ex-Sherry European oak casks, the former driving vanilla and dried stone fruit, the latter layering dried fig and dark spice across the longer contact time. At the blend's structural core sits Cardhu (Speyside) for honeyed fruit-forward sweetness, Caol Ila (Islay) for clean maritime peat, and Clynelish (Highland) for its distinctively waxy, coastal depth — a three-region architecture no single-distillery expression can replicate. The extended 18-year minimum is the special feature: where Black Label draws on 12-year minimums across roughly 40 whiskies, this expression demands six additional years of cask integration, softening Caol Ila's smoke into a background maritime register while amplifying the wax and dried-fruit complexity from Clynelish and the Sherry casks. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered to standard. Non-vintage by design — consistency across batches is the explicit goal of master blender Dr. Emma Walker.

name: Tomintoul  21 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description: 21 years in ex-Bourbon at 345 m altitude, Ballantruan Spring water, chill-filtered 40% ABV — Speyside's gentle giant, built on high-reflux restraint.
description:
Founded in 1964 by Glasgow whisky traders Hay & Macleod and W. & S. Strong, Tomintoul spent its first 35 years as near-invisible blending stock for Whyte & Mackay — a paradox for a distillery running 3.3 million litres annually. Angus Dundee's 2000 acquisition finally turned that volume into a single malt identity.

The distillery sits at approximately 345 m elevation in the Livet Glen within Cairngorms National Park, drawing water from the Ballantruan Spring in the Cromdale Hills — exceptionally soft and pure, a direct architectural input into the light, clean spirit character that defines the house.

The 21 Year Old is built on primary maturation in American white oak ex-Bourbon casks, the predominant vessel across the core range, with fermentation running 54–60 hours through stainless-steel washbacks and new make collected at ~69% ABV via steam-heated stills designed for high reflux. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, with E150a colouring — a transparency point worth noting at this price tier.

What distinguishes this expression within the core range is sheer time: two decades-plus in ex-Bourbon allows the soft, cereal-forward new make to develop layered vanilla and dried orchard depth without the distillery ever reaching for sherry-cask amplification.

Approachable now, with the structural patience of extended Speyside maturation behind it.

name: The Glenrothes  25 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description: Glenrothes Distillery, Rothes: 25 years in first-fill Jerez sherry butts, tall-still reflux, volcanic spring water, bottled 43% uncoloured.
description:
When Berry Bros. & Rudd began releasing Glenrothes by distillation year in 1993 — treating Scotch maturation with the same vintage logic applied to Bordeaux — they established a cult following that Edrington inherited when it reunited brand and distillery in 2017. The 25 Year Old is the age-statement expression that now carries that legacy forward.

The distillery sits in a sheltered glen beside the Burn of Rothes at the foot of the Mannoch Hills, drawing production water exclusively from four private on-estate springs naturally filtered through volcanic bedrock — a geological rarity in Speyside that contributes mineral clarity to the base spirit.

Distillation runs through five tall copper pot stills fitted with oversized boiling balls, deliberately operated at approximately half the flow rate of comparable Speyside distilleries to maximise copper reflux and build a bright, estery new make. Only the hearts cut — less than roughly 20% of total distillate — enters maturation. The spirit then spends 25 years predominantly in first-fill sherry-seasoned butts (European and American oak, up to 700 L, sourced from Jerez) held in traditional dunnage warehouses, with ex-bourbon barrels playing a secondary role. Bottled at 43% ABV, natural colour, non-chill-filtered.

What distinguishes this expression within the Speyside canon is the on-site cooperage — one of very few in Scotland — where four coopers repair approximately 6,000 casks annually, giving Glenrothes direct control over cask integrity across a quarter-century of maturation that peers simply cannot replicate.

name: The Glenrothes  Maker's Cut (700 ml)
short_description: Bottled at 48.8% ABV, non-chill-filtered — Glenrothes Maker's Cut draws from Jerez first-fill sherry butts at Rothes, spring-filtered through volcanic bedrock.
description: When Edrington reacquired The Glenrothes brand in 2017 and retired the cult vintage-dated format pioneered under Berry Bros. & Rudd, the Maker's Cut emerged as the house's declaration of intent: a no-compromise, 48.8% ABV, non-chill-filtered expression that preserves the full texture the distillery's unusually slow distillation rate produces. The distillery sits beside the Burn of Rothes at the foot of the Mannoch Hills, drawing water exclusively from four private on-estate springs — including the Ardcanny and Brauchhill sources — naturally filtered through volcanic bedrock, one of very few Scottish distilleries to control its own water supply from production through spirit reduction. That mineral-laden water feeds a process defined by tall copper pot stills with oversized boiling balls run at approximately half the flow rate of comparable Speyside operations, maximising copper reflux and generating a bright, estery new make from a hearts cut retaining less than ~20% of total distillate. Maturation leans on first-fill sherry-seasoned butts — both European and American oak, sourced from Jerez, up to 700 L capacity — held in traditional dunnage warehouses, with the distillery's own on-site cooperage (one of Scotland's rarest) repairing roughly 6,000 casks annually to maintain cask integrity. What separates this expression from the core age-statement lineup is the higher bottling strength and the absence of chill-filtration, which preserves the oily, full-textured mouthfeel the sherry-butt regime builds over time. Natural colour; no caramel addition.

name: Suntory  Yamazaki 25 Year Old Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
short_description: Japan's first malt distillery, 1923. Yamazaki 25 draws deep Mizunara oak influence — sandalwood, incense, agarwood — from one of whisky's most porous, difficult-to-cooperate woods.
description:
Shinjirō Torii broke a foundational orthodoxy in 1923 when he built Japan's first malt whisky distillery not in Scotland but at the misty foot of Mount Tennōzan — the same site where tea master Sen no Rikyū drew water four centuries earlier. That choice of location was never romantic; it was technical. Yamazaki's aquifer-fed water, softened by the confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers at ~25 m elevation, produces a new make of uncommon delicacy, while the warm, humid microclimate accelerates maturation in ways Scotland's cooler cellars cannot replicate.

At 25 years, the cask regime shifts decisively toward Japanese Mizunara oak (Quercus mongolica) — a wood so porous it demands near-constant cooperage attention and is used almost nowhere else in world whisky production. Extended contact with Mizunara over two-plus decades extracts the wood's signature sandalwood, incense, and kyara (agarwood) aromatics, layered over the darker fruit and spice contributed by ex-Oloroso sherry Spanish oak components. Distillation across Yamazaki's deliberately varied pot still shapes — squat, tall, and ball-necked, with both worm-tub and shell-and-tube condensers — ensures the blending team draws from a wide palette of malt styles, all produced in-house under the Tsukuriwake philosophy without inter-distillery trading.

What no peer replicates: the Mizunara influence at this age statement is not a finishing note but a structural presence — decades of slow extraction from a wood that resists cooperage and rewards patience with aromatics chemically distinct from any European oak.

name: The Macallan  A Night On Earth (700 ml)
short_description: Easter Elchies Estate NV release: sherry-seasoned oak drives a heavy, oily new make from Speyside's most upstream-integrated cask programme.
description:
The Macallan A Night On Earth is a limited, non-age-stated release from the Easter Elchies Estate — 485 acres of alluvial Speyside soil on the north-west slopes of the River Spey near Craigellachie, where on-site spring water feeds a 17-tonne lauter mash tun reportedly the largest in Scotland. Alluvial, fertile ground and a cool Moray microclimate support the estate's own barley programme, the grain mashed and fermented across 21 stainless-steel washbacks at approximately 55–60 hours before double distillation.

The production signature is the curiously small, squat spirit stills at just 3,900 litres — cast from death masks of the original historic stills by Forsyths of Rothes — which deliberately limit copper-vapour contact to yield a heavier, oilier new make built to absorb long sherry-cask maturation. Maturation draws on sherry-seasoned oak from The Macallan's vertically integrated cask programme: Spanish oak air-dried, coopered, and Oloroso-seasoned in Jerez for 18 months before filling, with the distillery controlling the process from forest to filled cask. Natural colour only — no E150a.

What distinguishes this expression within the Macallan portfolio is its gifting-tier presentation and NV blended character, designed as a sensory narrative rather than an age statement — a deliberate departure from the core range's year-anchored identity. Dried fig, dark chocolate, stem ginger, and warm baking spice emerge from the sherry-oak dominance that defines Easter Elchies.

name: Suntory  Yamazaki 18 years old Single Malt Whisky 100th Anniversary Limited Edition (700 ml)
short_description: Japan's first malt distillery marks its centennial with a Yamazaki 18 carrying amplified Mizunara oak — sandalwood, incense, and agarwood that no other wood on earth delivers.
description:
When Shinjirō Torii lit the stills at Yamazaki in 1923 — at the misty confluence of the Katsura, Uji, and Kizu rivers on the southwestern fringe of Kyoto — he drew water from the same aquifers that tea master Sen no Rikyū had prized four centuries earlier. One hundred years on, this anniversary release commemorates that founding act with the most Mizunara-forward iteration of Yamazaki 18 the distillery has produced for general release.

The site sits at roughly 25 metres elevation in a warm, humid microclimate that accelerates maturation relative to highland distilleries — the soft, low-mineral river-fed water dissolving oak compounds with unusual efficiency. Those conditions make extended Mizunara contact particularly expressive: Japanese Mizunara oak (Quercus mongolica), so porous it demands specialist cooperage and is used almost nowhere else in world whisky, imparts sandalwood, kyara incense, and agarwood aromatics that are chemically distinct from any European or American oak regime.

Distillation at Yamazaki runs through pot stills of deliberately varied shapes — squat, tall, and ball-necked — using both direct-fire and indirect heat, with worm-tub and shell-and-tube condensers in parallel, all to generate the breadth of malt styles the 'Tsukuriwake' philosophy demands. Fermentation spans 64–80 hours across Oregon pine wooden washbacks and stainless-steel vessels with multiple yeast strains. The 18-year age statement draws on American white oak ex-bourbon, Oloroso sherry casks, and a meaningfully elevated Mizunara component — the proportion of Mizunara increasing at each tier above Yamazaki 12.

What separates this 100th Anniversary edition within the Yamazaki 18 lineage is its commemorative positioning: a single-distillery expression bottled to mark the centennial of Japanese whisky itself, from the house whose Sherry Cask 2013 was named World's Best Whisky by Jim Murray's Whisky Bible — the release that triggered the global Japanese whisky boom.

name: Johnnie Walker  Blue Label Elusive Umami (750 ml)
short_description: Blue Label's rarest 1-in-10,000 casks, retuned by Master Blender Dr. Emma Walker to amplify umami-driven savory depth across Speyside, Islay, Highland, and Lowland.
description: When John Walker opened his Kilmarnock grocery in 1820, his tea-trade instinct — that blending across origins eliminates batch variance — became the founding logic of an entire whisky category. Two centuries later, Dr. Emma Walker, the first woman to hold the master blender title in the brand's history, applied that same principle to a deliberate sensory brief: isolate and amplify the savory, glutamate-rich register that Scotch whisky shares with aged proteins and fermented foods. The result is Blue Label Elusive Umami, assembled from hand-selected casks that qualify under Blue Label's strict 1-in-10,000 threshold — the most restrictive selection rate in the standard Johnnie Walker range. The blend draws on the house's four-pillar architecture: Cardhu (Speyside) for honeyed grain sweetness, Caol Ila (Islay) for clean maritime smoke, Clynelish (Highland) for waxy coastal depth, and Glenkinchie (Lowland) for floral lift — but cask selection here skews toward barrels that have developed pronounced meaty, brothy, and dried-mushroom character through extended ex-bourbon and ex-Sherry oak contact. Bottled at 40% ABV, chill-filtered, non-vintage. What distinguishes this expression within the Blue Label family is its explicit flavor-concept brief — a commercial blended Scotch engineered around a single taste sensation (umami) rather than a regional or age profile, a structural transgression for the category.

name: The Gladstone Axe - American Oak Blended Malt Scotch Whisky (700 ml)
Bruichladdich  Octomore 14.1 The Backbone Of The Series (700 ml)
short_description: Octomore 14.1: the world's most heavily peated malt, distilled on Islay's Rhinns peninsula, bottled cask-strength without chill-filtration or colouring.
description:
Octomore is the deliberate transgression at the heart of Bruichladdich's philosophy: the same stillhouse on the western shore of Loch Indaal that produces a fully unpeated flagship also generates the most heavily peated single malt Scotch whisky on earth — a contradiction that defines the distillery's identity since its 1881 founding by the Harvey brothers. The 14.1 sits at the backbone of that series, its barley peated to levels (150–309+ ppm range across the Octomore line) that dwarf any competitor, yet the spirit retains the coastal acidity and barley sweetness that the Rhinns microclimate consistently delivers.

Reduction water drawn from the Octomore Farm spring — flowing through ancient Lewisian gneiss rock at high silica content, unique on Islay — is used at bottling, contributing a mineral precision that separates Octomore from peat-forward peers. Distillation runs through two unusually tall, narrow-necked 6-metre spirit stills with shell-and-tube condensers, maximising copper reflux to produce a clean new make that carries peat smoke without coarseness. Maturation proceeds entirely on Islay in flat, brick-built dunnage warehouses under Atlantic-maritime conditions — Gulf Stream humidity slowing extraction and preserving barley character beneath the phenolic load. Bottled at cask strength, no chill-filtration, no caramel colouring, in bottles using ~60% recycled glass. The 14.1's special distinction: it is the entry-point 'backbone' expression of a series where every edition discloses full barley provenance — farm, field, variety, harvest year — a level of agricultural transparency virtually absent elsewhere in Scotch whisky.

name: Bruichladdich  Octomore 14.2 (Impeccable Balance Of Cask & Spirit) (700 ml)
short_description: Octomore 14.2: super-heavily peated Islay single malt finished in undisclosed wine casks, bottled unchill-filtered at cask strength from dunnage warehouses on the Rhinns.
description: Octomore is the expression that inverts every assumption about peat: distilled at 150–309+ ppm phenol — the highest peating level in commercial Scotch whisky — yet the Harvey brothers' 1881 stillhouse on the western shore of Loch Indaal was built for a completely different purpose. The 6-metre, narrow-necked spirit stills maximise copper reflux, producing a new make of unusual cleanliness that carries peat as texture and smoke rather than medicinal weight. The 14.2 designation signals the second cask variant of the fourteenth edition, where the distillery's deliberate wine-cask finishing regime — drawn from a diverse policy including French wine and fortified wine oak — is the defining technical variable, layering fruit-derived tannin and oxidative complexity over the phenolic core. Reduction to bottling strength uses water from the Octomore Farm spring, flowing through ancient Lewisian gneiss rock on the Rhinns, a high-silica source unique to this site. Matured entirely on Islay in flat, brick-built dunnage warehouses under Atlantic-maritime conditions — Gulf Stream humidity slowing extraction and preserving coastal salinity. No chill-filtration, no caramel colouring. What makes 14.2 categorically unusual: it achieves the world's highest commercial peat level while the wine-cask finish pulls the spirit toward dried fruit and spice, a structural tension no peer in the heavily peated category attempts at this scale.

name: Bruichladdich  30 Aged Years Old Whisky (700 ml)
short_description: Legacy stock from Whyte & Mackay-era Bruichladdich, exclusively ex-Bourbon matured 30 years on Islay, unpeated, unchill-filtered, 43% ABV.
description: The Bruichladdich 30 draws on spirit distilled during the Whyte & Mackay ownership era — the so-called 'resurrection legacy' stock laid down between 1989 and 1994, before the distillery's celebrated 2001 revival. That provenance matters: this is whisky made before the current team's radical terroir programme, barley-traceability obsession, or B Corp philosophy existed, yet it was shaped by the same physical infrastructure — the original 1881 cast-iron open rake-and-plough mash tun, six Douglas Fir wooden washbacks, and the unusually tall, narrow-necked (0.9m) spirit stills that maximise copper reflux and produce a characteristically clean, honeyed new make. Maturation is exclusively ex-Bourbon cask, conducted entirely on Islay in flat, brick-built dunnage warehouses where the Atlantic-maritime Gulf Stream climate — high humidity, minimal temperature swing — enforces a slow, gradual extraction over three decades. Reduction to 43% ABV uses water from the Octomore Farm spring, flowing through ancient Lewisian gneiss rock for a high-silica softness. No chill-filtration; no caramel colouring. What makes this expression categorically unusual: it is an unpeated Islay single malt aged 30 years — a combination the island's peat-forward reputation renders almost paradoxical — bottled at a restrained ABV that preserves the slow-built waxy, coastal complexity without oak dominance.

name: Bruichladdich  Octomore 13.1 Whisky (700 ml)
short_description: Octomore 13.1: 2015 Islay barley, 137.3 ppm peat, cask-strength, unchillfiltered — the world's most heavily peated single malt, distilled at sea level on Loch Indaal.
description: Octomore is the deliberate transgression at the heart of Bruichladdich's philosophy: the Harvey brothers built this distillery in 1881 on the western shore of Loch Indaal for gravity-fed, pump-free production, and over a century later the same stillhouse now produces both a fully unpeated flagship and the world's most heavily peated single malt — a contradiction no other Islay distillery attempts. The 13.1 edition is distilled from 2015-harvest Scottish barley peated to 137.3 ppm, malted off-island at Bairds Malt, Inverness, then returned to the Rhinns of Islay for production. The Atlantic-maritime climate at near sea level — Gulf Stream-moderated, rarely freezing, with high year-round humidity — slows maturation inside flat, brick-built dunnage warehouses, allowing the spirit to integrate rather than accelerate. Distillation passes through two unusually tall, narrow-necked spirit stills (6-metre column, 0.9m neck diameter), maximising copper reflux and producing a cleaner new make than the ppm figure alone would suggest. Reduction uses water drawn from the Octomore Farm spring, flowing through ancient Lewisian gneiss rock — a high-silica source unique to this corner of the island. Bottled at cask strength, without chill-filtration and without caramel colouring. The special feature: at 137.3 ppm, this expression carries more phenolic load than any other commercially released single malt category, yet the distillery's inherent coastal acidity and barley sweetness remain structurally present beneath the smoke.

name: The Macallan  A Night On Earth In Jerez De La Frontera (700 ml)
short_description: Speyside single malt finished in Jerez-seasoned Oloroso casks — part of Macallan's artist series celebrating the sherry capital that built its identity.
description: The A Night On Earth In Jerez De La Frontera release belongs to The Macallan's artist-collaboration series, anchoring its storytelling directly to Jerez — the Andalusian city whose Oloroso-seasoned European oak casks are the structural foundation of the entire Macallan house style. That relationship is not decorative: Macallan operates one of whisky's most vertically integrated cask programmes, sourcing Spanish oak from Galicia, Asturias, and Cantabria, air-drying staves for approximately one year, coopering them in Spain, and seasoning the finished casks in Jerez for 18 months with Oloroso sherry before a single drop of spirit enters. The new make itself is engineered for this wood: squat copper pot spirit stills of just 3,900 litres — craft-distillery scale — limit copper-vapour contact and produce a deliberately heavy, oily spirit capable of absorbing decades of sherry-cask influence. Distilled at Easter Elchies Estate on the north-west slopes of the River Spey near Craigellachie, using on-site spring water and unpeated malted barley, the spirit matures across 51 on-site warehouses in 100% ex-Oloroso sherry-seasoned European oak. What distinguishes this expression within the Macallan portfolio is its dual function: a collectible artist release that simultaneously documents the Jerez origin story most Speyside producers outsource entirely. Natural colour only; no added caramel (E150a).

name: Glen Scotia  18 Year Old Single Malt Whisky (700 ml)
Boutique-y Bunnahabhain Aged 27 Years - Bangkok Skyline Series Vol 1 (500 ml)
short_description: 27-year Bunnahabhain from Islay's least-peated distillery, bottled at cask strength — no colour, no chill filtration — by TBWC's cult independent house.
description: When That Boutique-y Whisky Company launched in September 2012, two of its four debut releases were from long-closed distilleries — a deliberate signal that cask rarity and liquid integrity would always outrank category convention. This 27-year expression from Bunnahabhain is a product of that same philosophy: a single cask or small-batch selection made by Head of Whisky Sam Simmons, drawn from Islay's northernmost distillery, which sits on the Sound of Islay facing the Paps of Jura. Bunnahabhain's position — exposed to Atlantic salt air yet sheltered enough to allow slow, cool maturation — produces spirit that diverges sharply from Islay's peated mainstream. No artificial colouring and no chill filtration are non-negotiable house standards, preserving every ester and fatty acid accumulated across nearly three decades in cask. At 27 years, the oak regime — almost certainly ex-bourbon American oak given the distillery's standard fill policy — has had time to layer dried fruit, beeswax, and sea-salted toffee without overwhelming Bunnahabhain's characteristically unpeated, maritime-driven new make. The special feature here is the tension itself: Islay provenance with no peat, aged long enough that the wood conversation dominates yet the coastal salinity never fully retreats. The Bangkok Skyline Series Vol 1 designation marks this as a market-specific, limited release — batch sizes across the TBWC range run to approximately 1,000 bottles — making replication impossible.

name: Glenfiddich Grand Chateau 31 Years Old (700 ml)
short_description: 31 years in Speyside oak, then 9 years finishing in Bordeaux wine casks — the longest cask finish in the Glenfiddich Grand Series.
description: William Grant built Glenfiddich by hand in 1886, sourcing every stone from the Conval Hills above Dufftown — the same hills that shelter the Robbie Dhu Spring, the sole water source used from mash to reduction. That geological specificity matters here: the Grand Château's base spirit spent its first decades in predominantly ex-bourbon American white oak before entering a 9-year finish in Bordeaux wine casks, the longest finishing regime in the entire Grand Series and a duration that crosses from 'finish' into something closer to a second maturation. The Conval Hills valley microclimate — cooler and more stable than open Speyside — slows extraction in the stone-walled dunnage warehouses, preserving the distillery's signature swan-neck still delicacy even across three decades. Those small pear-shaped wash stills (~9,000 L) and spirit stills (~4,500 L), maintained by in-house coppersmiths to preserve exact shape consistency, produce a light, high-ester new make that carries fruit character through extended oak contact without being overwhelmed. The Bordeaux cask influence — tannin structure, dried dark fruit, subtle graphite — sits in direct tension with Glenfiddich's house lightness, producing an internal contradiction that no standard Speyside 30-year-old replicates. Bottled at Dufftown, reduced with Robbie Dhu spring water; filtration status at this tier not confirmed from official sources.

name: Singleton of Glen Ord 17 Year Old (700 ml) (F26 Special Releases 2025)
Lagavulin 12 Year Old (700 ml) (F26 Special Releases 2025)
short_description: Islay's slowest distillation, bottled cask-strength from refill American oak — the 12YO Special Release strips Lagavulin to its densest, most phenolic core.
description:
When Peter Mackie won the legal battle for exclusive rights to the Lochan Sholum burn — peat-stained water descending 200 metres from the hillside of Bheinn Sholum — he secured not just a water source but the chemical backbone of Lagavulin's character. That same water feeds the 2025 Special Release, the annual cask-strength iteration of the 12 Year Old that has no permanent counterpart in the core range.

The distillery sits directly on Lagavulin Bay, Kildalton Coast, where dunnage warehouses absorb constant Atlantic sea-spray, pressing iodine and brine into every cask during maturation. The spirit inside those casks is the heaviest produced on Islay: wash stills filled to 95% capacity (versus an industry norm of 80–85%) and a spirit run lasting up to nine hours — the slowest on the island — deliberately suppress copper contact, retaining dense phenolic and tarry congeners at ≈35–38 PPM in the glass.

The 12 Year Old Special Release matures exclusively in refill American white oak ex-bourbon casks, bottled at cask strength (typically 56–58% ABV), non-chill-filtered — no caramel addition, no dilution. Where the flagship 16 Year Old softens Lagavulin's phenolic density with sherry-butt integration and additional years, this expression delivers the same architecture at full voltage, the tarry smoke and coastal iodine unmediated by sweetness.

No reliable batch-specific tasting data exists for the F26 2025 release; drinking window is broad given cask-strength bottling — best opened 2025–2032.

name: Oban 12 Year Old (700 ml) (F26 Special Releases 2025)
Clynelish 18 Year Old (700 ml) (F26 Special Releases 2025)
Roseisle 14 Year Old (700 ml) (F26 Special Releases 2025)
Dailuaine 21 Year Old (700 ml) (F26 Special Releases 2025)
Teaninich 8 Year Old (700 ml) (F26 Special Releases 2025)
The GlenAllachie 18 Years old (700 ml)
short_description: Speyside built at the foot of Ben Rinnes — 160-hr fermentation, first/second-fill sherry puncheons, non-chill filtered at 46% ABV.
description: For its first five decades, GlenAllachie was an anonymous blending workhorse supplying Chivas Regal and Clan Campbell — virtually no whisky left the distillery under its own name. When Billy Walker acquired the site in 2017, he slashed output by roughly 87% and rebuilt the programme around one of Speyside's most aggressive cask strategies. Situated at the foot of Ben Rinnes near Aberlour, Banffshire, the distillery draws process water from Blackstank and Heanshead Dams, fed by spring- and snowmelt-fed streams on Ben Rinnes's north-east slopes — cold, mineral-rich water that supports a clean, ester-forward new make. Fermentation runs 160–164 hours in 60,000-litre stainless steel washbacks — among Scotland's longest standard cycles — deliberately maximising ester formation before double distillation through lantern-shaped wash stills and horizontal shell-and-tube condensers. The 18 Year Old draws on inherited pre-2017 stocks matured under a first- and second-fill only policy, with the cask palette anchored by Pedro Ximénez and Oloroso puncheons alongside ex-Bourbon American oak, all bottled at 46% ABV, non-chill filtered, natural colour. What separates this expression from Speyside peers is the sheer density of sherry-cask contact on spirit that was never designed to be bottled as single malt — the result is a muscular, dark-fruit-driven profile that sits closer to a Sherry-region Highland than a conventional Speyside. Drinking well now through the mid-2030s.

name: The GlenAllachie 21 Years old (700 ml)
short_description: 50-year blending workhorse reinvented: 21YO Speyside from Ben Rinnes, non-chill filtered, first/second-fill Sherry & Bourbon casks only.
description: For its first five decades, GlenAllachie was the anonymous backbone of Chivas Regal and Clan Campbell — virtually no whisky left the Aberlour site with its own name on the label. When Billy Walker acquired the distillery in 2017, he slashed output by roughly 87% and imposed a strict first- and second-fill cask-only policy, ensuring every drop of the inherited stock would be re-evaluated on its merits. The 21 Year Old draws on that pre-2017 spirit, now matured under Walker's watch at the foot of Ben Rinnes, where spring- and snowmelt-fed streams from the north-east slopes supply the process water via Blackstank and Heanshead Dams — cold, mineral-rich water that contributes to the distillery's characteristically dense, fruit-laden new make. That new make is itself built on a 160–164 hour fermentation in 60,000-litre washbacks — among Scotland's longest standard fermentation windows — deliberately engineered to maximise ester formation before double distillation through lantern-shaped wash stills and horizontal shell-and-tube condensers. At 21 years, the spirit has spent extended time across a portfolio anchored by ex-Pedro Ximénez and Oloroso Sherry puncheons alongside ex-Bourbon American oak, bottled at a minimum 46% ABV, non-chill filtered and natural colour. What sets this expression apart within Speyside's 21-year tier is the combination of inherited pre-Walker distillate — lighter and more ester-forward from its blending-era regime — reshaped entirely by Walker's aggressive Sherry cask programme, producing a structural tension no peer distillery can replicate. Drinking well now, with the density to reward further cellaring through the late 2020s.

name: The GlenAllachie Sinteis Series 2014 Chinquapin & PX Cask Matured (700 ml)
short_description: 2014 Speyside single malt finished in Chinquapin virgin oak & PX casks — Billy Walker's most unconventional wood pairing in the Sinteis series.
description: When Billy Walker acquired GlenAllachie in 2017, he inherited a warehouse of pre-Walker-era spirit distilled during the Chivas Brothers blending era — stocks that had spent their formative years in anonymous service. The Sinteis Series exists to give those casks a singular identity through deliberate, unconventional wood intervention. This 2014 expression pairs Chinquapin virgin oak — a white oak subspecies native to the American South, prized for its tighter grain and higher vanillin yield than standard American oak — with Pedro Ximénez puncheons, the same variety that anchors over 30% of GlenAllachie's total maturation programme. The distillery sits at the foot of Ben Rinnes near Aberlour, Banffshire, drawing process water from spring- and snowmelt-fed streams via Blackstank and Heanshead Dams; that cold, mineral-clean water underpins the fruity, muscular new make produced through 160–164 hour fermentation — among Scotland's longest standard fermentation windows, engineered specifically to maximise ester formation. Bottled at natural colour, non-chill filtered, and at a minimum 46% ABV, the Sinteis 2014 is unusual within Speyside for deploying Chinquapin oak, a cask type virtually absent from Scottish maturation practice, layering coconut-rich virgin wood tannins directly against the raisin-dense sweetness of PX. Drink now through 2032.

Primary notes of red fruit and dark plum are complemented by black pepper and spice on the palate, with herbaceous and earthy undertones that reflect the Southern Rhône terroir. Smoke and stone fruit notes add complexity. Food-friendly and versatile, this blend suits grilled meats, Mediterranean dishes, and tomato-based preparations.

name: Tyrrell's  Shiraz "Moore's Creek"
short_description: Hunter Valley Shiraz with dark plum, black pepper, and smoky spice—a food-friendly, medium-full red.
description: Tyrrell's Moore's Creek Shiraz is a classic Hunter Valley expression of Australia's signature varietal. The wine opens with dominant dark plum and blackberry fruit, layered with black pepper and smoke that reflect the region's warm climate and the varietal's natural spice. Medium-full body and balanced tannins make this wine approachable yet structured, with emerging leather and earthy notes that suggest modest aging potential. A versatile red suited to grilled meats, braised beef, and smoky barbecue.

name: Tyrrell's  Cabernet Sauvignon "Moore's Creek"
short_description: Hunter Valley Cabernet Sauvignon with ripe blackcurrant, cedar spice, and structured tannins—a food-friendly classic.
description: Tyrrell's Moore's Creek Cabernet Sauvignon is a Hunter Valley expression of Australia's signature red varietal. The wine opens with dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, supported by secondary cedar and black pepper notes that reflect both fruit ripeness and subtle oak influence. A medium-full body and balanced tannin structure provide the framework for food pairing versatility, while emerging earth and leather notes suggest modest cellaring potential. This is a straightforward, well-made Cabernet suited to grilled red meat, braised beef, and herb-forward lamb dishes.

name: Tyrrell's  Shiraz "Old Winery"
short_description: Hunter Valley Shiraz with ripe dark fruit, peppery spice, and smoky complexity—a food-friendly classic.
description: Tyrrell's Old Winery Shiraz represents the Hunter Valley's signature expression of this varietal. The wine opens with dominant dark plum and blackberry fruit, layered with black pepper spice and subtle smoke that reflect both fruit ripeness and winemaking craft. Medium-full body and balanced tannin structure provide approachable power without heaviness, while emerging leather and earthy notes add complexity on the finish. A versatile red suited to grilled meats, braised dishes, and smoked preparations.

name: Millaman Estate Reserve Cabernet Sauvignon Malbec
Tahuna  Pinot Noir Hawke's Bay
short_description: Elegant Hawke's Bay Pinot Noir with bright red fruit, silky tannins, and fresh acidity—a versatile summer wine.
description: Tahuna's Pinot Noir from Hawke's Bay showcases the region's signature elegance and restraint. Bright red fruit and raspberry dominate the nose and palate, supported by subtle earthy notes and a whisper of spice. The wine's light body and silky tannin structure make it approachable and food-friendly, while high acidity provides freshness and lift—ideal for warm-weather drinking and a wide range of culinary pairings.

name: Caliterra  Edicion Limitada A
short_description: Carmenere-led Colchagua blend with dark fruit, spice, and structured tannins—elegant Chilean red for premium dining.
description: Caliterra's Edición Limitada A is a carefully composed Colchagua Valley blend anchored by Carmenere (65%), with supporting Malbec (32%) and Petit Verdot (3%). The wine displays a medium-full body with dark plum and blackcurrant on the nose, layered with cedar and black pepper. Mid-palate tannins are structured and refined, with cocoa and tobacco notes emerging on the finish. This is a food-friendly yet sophisticated red suited to grilled meats, braised dishes, and aged cheeses.

name: Heitz Cellar  Martha's Vineyard Cabernet Sauvignon
St. Hallett  Barossa Faith Shiraz
short_description: Barossa Shiraz with ripe dark fruit, spice, and oak—a food-friendly, medium-full red for fine dining.
description: St. Hallett's Barossa Faith Shiraz delivers the signature warmth and richness of the region's premier Shiraz. Ripe blackberry and plum dominate the palate, supported by layers of vanilla oak, cocoa, and black pepper. A subtle smoke character adds complexity and age-worthiness. Medium-full bodied with balanced tannins, this wine bridges approachability with structure—equally at home at the dinner table or cellared for further development.

name: Feudi Del Pisciotto  Missoni Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Sicilian Cabernet Sauvignon with ripe blackcurrant, cedar, and structured tannins—elegant and food-friendly.
description: A single-varietal Cabernet Sauvignon from Sicily's Feudi Del Pisciotto, this 2017 vintage expresses the grape's classic dark-fruit character with blackcurrant and black cherry at the fore. Cedar and black pepper notes from winemaking add complexity and structure, while a medium-full body and balanced tannins make it an approachable yet serious red. Herbaceous undertones and emerging tertiary earth and tobacco suggest graceful aging potential. Ideal for grilled red meat, lamb, and aged cheeses.

name: Dominus Estate  2013
short_description: Dry-farmed Napanook Vineyard, Yountville — Christian Moueix's Left Bank discipline applied to volcanic alluvial gravel, 2013 vintage.
description: Christian Moueix, steward of Château Pétrus in Pomerol, arrived in Napa Valley in 1982 not to make a Napa wine but to make a Moueix wine — estate-bottled, single-vineyard, unirrigated — at a moment when the valley was racing toward extraction and heavy new oak. That philosophical transplant defines every bottle of Dominus. Napanook Vineyard spans 108 planted acres on an alluvial fan descending from the Mayacamas foothills in Yountville AVA; the upper blocks contributing Dominus fruit sit on the coolest, highest-elevation gravel soils with volcanic clay-loam beneath, delivering natural drainage and forcing root systems deep into fractured subsoil. The estate is entirely dry-farmed — virtually unheard of in Napa Valley — relying on underground springs and rainfall alone, producing small, phenolically concentrated berries. Vinification proceeds block by block, with hand-sorting augmented by optical berry selection, gentle pump-overs, and a low-pressure vertical press to preserve tannin finesse. Aging runs 15–18 months in 100% French oak barrels, with roughly one-third to 40% new wood depending on vintage — restrained by Napa standards. The 2013 vintage, not individually documented in producer materials, falls within the estate's signature graphite-and-dark-currant register, with the iron-spined structure and tobacco depth that routinely demands a decade of cellaring before full expression. Drink from 2025 through the late 2030s.

short_description: Carmenere-led Gran Reserva from Colchagua Valley with dark fruit, spice, and structured tannins—elegant Chilean classic.
description: Caliterra's Carmenere Tributo Gran Reserva is a classically structured Chilean red anchored by 98% Carmenere, with supporting Malbec and Petit Verdot. The wine displays ripe dark plum and blackcurrant on the nose, layered with cedar, black pepper, and subtle cocoa from careful oak aging. Medium-full body and medium-high tannins provide backbone without heaviness, making this a food-friendly expression of Colchagua Valley terroir. The tertiary tobacco and leather notes suggest modest bottle age potential and versatility across grilled meats, braised dishes, and game.

name: Los Vascos  Le Dix de Los Vascos
short_description: Elegant Bordeaux blend from Colchagua Valley; red fruit, cedar, and mineral complexity in a food-friendly frame.
description: Los Vascos Le Dix de Los Vascos is a Bordeaux-style red blend from Chile's Colchagua Valley, crafted by the prestigious Domaines Barons de Rothschild. The 2021 vintage showcases ripe red and dark fruit on the nose, with supporting notes of cedar and subtle oak that reflect careful winemaking. Medium-full body and balanced tannin structure make this wine versatile at the table—equally at home with grilled meats, braised dishes, or herb-forward preparations. A mineral undertone adds complexity and length to the finish.

name: Earthworks  Shiraz (Barossa Valley)
short_description: Full-bodied Barossa Shiraz with ripe blackberry, pepper spice, and smoky oak—a fine-dining red for grilled meats.
description: Earthworks Shiraz from Barossa Valley delivers a full-bodied expression of Australia's signature varietal. Ripe blackberry and plum dominate the palate, supported by black pepper spice and vanilla oak from careful winemaking. Smoke and chocolate notes add complexity and depth, making this wine a versatile companion to grilled red meat, lamb, and slow-cooked braises. The medium acidity and medium-high tannins provide structure without overwhelming, positioning this as an approachable yet serious red for fine dining and special occasions.

name: Chateau de Tourteyron  Medoc Cru Bourgeois
Viu Manent  Viu 1
short_description: Colchagua Malbec with dark fruit intensity, balanced oak, and food-friendly structure—a versatile Chilean red.
description: Viu Manent's Viu 1 is a 100% Malbec from Chile's Colchagua Valley, a region celebrated for producing ripe, structured red wines. The 2019 vintage delivers dominant dark plum and blackberry character on the nose and palate, supported by subtle spice and oak integration. Medium-full body and moderate tannin structure make this wine approachable in youth while retaining enough complexity for short-term cellaring. Earthy undertones and hints of tobacco emerge on the finish, adding depth to an otherwise fruit-forward profile.

name: Viu Manent  Single Vineyard Malbec
short_description: Colchagua Malbec with dark plum, spice, and leather—a food-friendly Chilean red for grilled meats and braised dishes.
description: Viu Manent's Single Vineyard Malbec from Colchagua Valley showcases the depth and structure characteristic of Chilean Malbec. The 2019 vintage delivers dominant dark plum and black cherry fruit on the nose, supported by cocoa and subtle spice from careful winemaking. A medium-full body and balanced tannin structure make this wine approachable yet age-worthy, with emerging leather and earth notes that suggest complexity. Ideal for grilled red meat, braised beef, and lamb dishes, this expression balances fruit-forward appeal with the mineral and savory character that defines quality Malbec.

name: Viu Manent  Gran Reserva Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Colchagua Cabernet-led blend with dark fruit, cedar, and structured tannin—food-friendly Chilean classic.
description: Viu Manent's Gran Reserva Cabernet Sauvignon is a Colchagua Valley expression built on 97% Cabernet Sauvignon, with Malbec and Petit Verdot adding depth and complexity. The wine opens with dominant blackcurrant and black cherry fruit, supported by cedar and cocoa notes from careful oak aging. Medium-full body and balanced tannin structure make it an approachable yet serious red, suitable for grilled meats, braised beef, and aged cheeses. The tertiary tobacco and leather notes emerge with time, rewarding patient cellaring.

name: Viu Manent  Gran Reserva Carmenere
short_description: Full-bodied Chilean Carmenere with dark fruit, spice, and elegant structure—a food-friendly classic from Colchagua Valley.
description:
Viu Manent's Gran Reserva Carmenere represents the signature expression of Chile's flagship red varietal. This medium-full bodied wine showcases the characteristic dark fruit intensity and peppery spice that define Carmenere from the Colchagua Valley, one of Chile's premier growing regions.

On the palate, layers of dark plum and black cherry are complemented by cocoa and herbaceous notes, with a structured tannin backbone that suggests food compatibility across grilled meats, braised dishes, and aged cheeses. The wine's tertiary tobacco and leather notes emerge with air, adding complexity suitable for both immediate enjoyment and modest cellaring.

name: Viu Manent  Rayuela Gran Reserva Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Colchagua Cabernet-led blend with dark fruit, cedar, and structured tannin—elegant Chilean classic for red meat.
description: Viu Manent's Rayuela Gran Reserva is a Cabernet Sauvignon–dominant blend from Chile's Colchagua Valley, built on a foundation of blackcurrant and dark plum fruit with supporting cedar and cocoa complexity. Medium-full body and medium-high tannin structure provide grip and aging potential, while tertiary tobacco and leather notes add sophistication. A food-friendly, classically styled red suited to grilled beef, braised meats, and aged cheeses.

name: Angove  Family Crest Mclaren Vale Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Organic McLaren Vale Cabernet with ripe dark berries, soft tannins, and Mediterranean warmth—food-friendly elegance.
description: Angove Family Crest Cabernet Sauvignon from McLaren Vale showcases the winery's mastery of organic viticulture in South Australia's coastal Mediterranean climate. The wine opens with vibrant dark cherry and blackcurrant fruit, supported by subtle earthiness and a supple tannin structure that speaks to careful winemaking. Medium-full bodied and approachable, it delivers the signature Angove profile: ripe fruit intensity balanced by elegant restraint, making it an ideal companion to grilled meats, braised dishes, and hearty Mediterranean fare.

name: Yalumba  Individuals Galway Vintage Traditional Shiraz
short_description: Barossa floor Shiraz from Australia's oldest family winery, wild-fermented in century-old open-top vessels and aged in hand-crafted Southern Hemisphere cooperage oak.
description: Founded in 1849 by Samuel Smith and still held by the sixth-generation Hill-Smith family, Yalumba operates the only winery-owned cooperage in the Southern Hemisphere — a fact that shapes every barrel this Barossa Shiraz ever touches. The Galway Vintage Traditional Shiraz draws from the warm, low-elevation Barossa Valley floor, where deep alluvial soils with clay subsoil retain enough moisture to sustain dry-grown vines through the region's fierce summer heat, concentrating fruit without sacrificing mid-palate density. Fermentation begins spontaneously with wild/native yeast in open-top fermenters, some of which date to the 1800s, a deliberate retention of microbial complexity that cultured-yeast programs cannot replicate. Oak aging follows in barrels sourced or hand-crafted on-site from French, American, and Hungarian staves, giving Yalumba a degree of oak-flavour calibration no peer in Australia can match. What sets this expression apart within the Yalumba range is its explicit nod to the traditional Barossa style — full-bodied, generously fruited, and built for the cellar rather than immediate approachability. As a current-vintage release, it delivers the plush, sun-warmed character Barossa Shiraz is known for while carrying the structural backbone that rewards 3–7 years of bottle age.

Yering Station  Yarra Valley Shiraz Viognier
short_description: Yarra Valley Shiraz co-fermented with 3% Viognier — wild yeast, hand-plunged, cool-climate structure over extraction.
description: Yering Station's Shiraz Viognier draws on a winemaking decision that is deliberate and structural: a small addition of Viognier is co-fermented alongside Shiraz — not blended after the fact — so the aromatic compounds from Viognier's skins bond directly with Shiraz's tannins during primary fermentation, softening grip without sacrificing definition. This is the same technique used in the northern Rhône, applied here to the cool southern slopes of the Yarra Valley. The Yarra Valley's volcanic loam and well-drained gravels, at elevations between 50 and 400 metres under maritime cool-climate influence, produce Shiraz that achieves phenolic ripeness well ahead of sugar ripeness — the estate's stated priority — yielding structure and length rather than jammy weight. Wild yeast fermentation and hand-plunging during primary fermentation extract tannin gently; French oak at restrained new-oak percentages amplifies the valley's natural minerality rather than overlaying it. What sets this expression apart within Australian Shiraz is the co-fermentation technique at this price tier — a practice almost exclusively reserved for Reserve-level bottlings elsewhere. The current vintage reflects the Yarra Valley's cool-climate character: taut, site-driven, and built for the medium term.

name: Merryvale  Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Napa Valley Cabernet-led blend with dark cherry, blackcurrant, and cedar; structured tannins and food-friendly balance.
description: Merryvale's 2018 Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon is a classically proportioned Bordeaux blend anchored by 77% Cabernet Sauvignon, with supporting Merlot, Cabernet Franc, and Malbec. The wine opens with dominant blackcurrant and black cherry fruit, layered with dark plum and subtle spice. Oak aging contributes vanilla, cedar, and cocoa undertones, while the mid-palate reveals structured tannins and balanced acidity. Tertiary notes of leather and tobacco suggest modest aging potential. A versatile, food-friendly expression of Napa Valley red wine tradition.

name: Staglin Family Vineyard  Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Single-estate Rutherford Bench Cab aged 22–24 months in 90–100% new French oak, from 51 certified-organic acres at the foot of Mount St. John.
description: When the Staglin family purchased this parcel in 1985, they inherited viticultural ground with roots stretching to the mid-1860s and immediately placed it under organic stewardship — a commitment formalised by CCOF certification in 2005 that remains rare at this prestige tier on the Rutherford Bench. The 51-acre estate sits on an alluvial fan at the base of Mount St. John, the tallest peak in the Mayacamas Range, where millennia of erosion have deposited deep, slightly gravelly, volcanic-origin soils with exceptional drainage. That drainage, combined with sun-drenched days and cool evenings pulled off San Pablo Bay, is the engine behind the site's signature: concentrated dark fruit threaded with the chalky, earthy grip Rutherford winemakers call 'Rutherford dust.' Grapes are harvested in cool early mornings, hand-sorted, destemmed, and fermented across stainless steel, concrete vats, and French oak barrels in a gravity-flow underground facility powered entirely by on-site solar energy. The wine then spends 22–24 months in 90–100% new French oak barriques, building the fine-grained tannin architecture that defines the estate's long-aging profile. What separates this from virtually every other Rutherford estate of comparable standing is the absolute refusal to source outside the family's own 61 acres — every bottle is a single-vineyard statement, not a valley blend. The 2018 vintage delivered even, warm ripening conditions across Napa, yielding wines of deep concentration and structural precision; drinking well now through the mid-2030s with proper cellaring.

name: Merryvale  Forward Kidd Napa
short_description: Napa Valley Rhône-style blend of Petite Sirah, Mouvedre, Syrah & Malbec. Dark fruit, spice, and structured tannins.
description:
Merryvale's Forward Kidd is a Napa Valley proprietary red blend anchored by Petite Sirah (47%), with supporting roles from Mouvedre (19%), Syrah (17%), and Malbec (17%). This Rhône-inspired composition delivers a medium-full bodied wine with medium-high tannins and medium acidity, built for food pairing and cellaring.

On the palate, dark plum and black cherry dominate the primary layer, supported by blackberry and white pepper spice. Secondary notes of leather and tobacco add complexity and structure. The wine shows earthy undertones characteristic of Napa Valley terroir, with a dry finish and firm tannin grip suitable for grilled meats, braised beef, and aged cheeses.

name: Mollydooker  'The Boxer' Shiraz
short_description: Full-bodied McLaren Vale Shiraz with ripe blackberry, plum, and mocha—soft tannins and juicy dark fruit.
description:
Mollydooker 'The Boxer' Shiraz 2021 is a full-bodied red from McLaren Vale, Australia's premier Shiraz region. The wine displays dominant ripe blackberry and dark plum on the nose, with supporting mocha and cocoa notes from oak aging. Black pepper spice and vanilla add complexity, while soft, integrated tannins provide structure without harshness. The palate is juicy and fruit-forward, with a warm, lingering finish.

A versatile food wine suited to grilled steaks, braised beef, lamb, and smoky BBQ. Also pairs well with aged cheeses and cured meats.

name: Mollydooker  ' Two Left Feet' Shiraz Cabernet Merlot
short_description: Ripe, fruit-forward McLaren Vale blend of Shiraz, Merlot & Cabernet with dark plum, spice & soft tannins.
description: Mollydooker's Two Left Feet is a medium-full bodied red blend anchored by Shiraz (69%), with supporting Merlot (16%) and Cabernet Sauvignon (15%). From McLaren Vale's warm climate, this wine delivers dominant dark plum and black cherry on the nose, with secondary vanilla oak and spice complexity. The palate is ripe and approachable, with soft tannins and balanced acidity that make it food-friendly across grilled meats, braised dishes, and rich terrines. A commercial, well-crafted blend suited to casual dining and entertaining.

name: Mollydooker  'Gigglepot' Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: Full-bodied McLaren Vale Cabernet with ripe blackcurrant, cedar oak, and structured tannins.
description: Mollydooker's Gigglepot Cabernet Sauvignon from McLaren Vale showcases the region's signature richness and power. The 2020 vintage delivers a full-bodied expression with dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, layered with cedary oak and black pepper spice. Structured tannins provide grip and aging potential, while tertiary notes of tobacco and leather add complexity. A wine built for the table—pair with grilled steaks, braised lamb, or aged hard cheeses.

name: Mollydooker  'Blue Eyed Boy' Shiraz
short_description: Full-bodied McLaren Vale Shiraz with ripe blackberry, plum, and mocha—soft tannins and spiced dark fruit intensity.
description: Mollydooker 'Blue Eyed Boy' Shiraz 2021 is a full-bodied expression from McLaren Vale showcasing the region's signature ripe dark fruit character. Blackberry and plum dominate the primary aromatics, supported by mocha and vanilla notes from oak maturation. The palate reveals soft, integrated tannins and a spiced dark fruit core, with subtle leather and earth undertones adding complexity. This is a generous, approachable Shiraz built for immediate enjoyment alongside hearty red meat dishes and grilled preparations.

name: Henschke  Keyneton Euphonium, Barossa Valley
short_description: Barossa Shiraz-Cabernet blend from a sixth-generation estate farming organically since 1862 — open-top concrete fermentation, no added tannins, full malolactic.
description: When Cyril Henschke bottled Australia's first single-vineyard Shiraz in 1952, the country's fine-wine identity was still defined by blends and fortifieds. Keyneton Euphonium is Henschke's deliberate return to that blending tradition — Shiraz, Cabernet Sauvignon, Cabernet Franc, and Merlot drawn from organically and biodynamically farmed Barossa Valley fruit, a counterpoint to the estate's celebrated single-varietal flagships. The Barossa floor sites deliver the warm-climate concentration the Eden Valley expressly avoids: deeper alluvial soils amplify dark fruit density while the Cabernet varieties contribute structural backbone and mid-palate definition. Fermentation takes place in open-top vessels with submerged-cap technique; full malolactic fermentation is carried through, and Stephen Henschke's standing rule of no added tannins means every structural element is native to the fruit. Aging follows the house hogshead regime in French and American oak, seasoned casks dominant, preserving fruit clarity over oak imprint. What sets Euphonium apart within the Barossa blend category is the Cabernet Franc component — a variety almost never seen in floor-Barossa reds — lending lifted graphite and dried herb character that cuts through the region's characteristic plush weight. The 2019 vintage across Henschke's portfolio was shaped by record 44.3°C heat and yields reduced to 10–15% of normal, producing wines of concentrated purity and tighter structure than the house average; drink 2024–2032.

name: Rockford  Basket Press Shiraz
short_description: Full-bodied Barossa Shiraz with ripe blackberry, plum, and smoky oak—a classic Australian red for grilled meats.
description: Rockford's 2018 Basket Press Shiraz from Barossa Valley is a full-bodied expression of Australia's signature varietal. Dominant blackberry and plum fruit drive the primary impression, supported by vanilla and oak from winemaking. Smoke and black pepper emerge on the finish, adding complexity and structure. Medium-high tannins and acidity provide grip and food-friendliness, making this wine a natural partner for grilled red meat, lamb, and slow-braised beef dishes.

name: Rockford  Moppa Springs Grenache Shiraz Mataro
Yalumba  Rare & Fine The Caley Cabernet & Shiraz
short_description: Coonawarra terra rossa Cabernet meets Barossa Shiraz, aged ~21 months in 41% new French oak — made only in declared vintages.
description: The Caley exists only when Yalumba's winemaking team declares a vintage worthy — a deliberate scarcity decision that has defined this inter-regional blend since its inception, continuing the house's tradition of Cabernet–Shiraz blending that stretches back to 1962. Coonawarra Cabernet Sauvignon (~55–69%) is drawn from the Menzies Vineyard, where terra rossa topsoil over limestone delivers the variety's characteristic graphite-edged structure and precise cassis fruit; the limestone drainage keeps vine stress calibrated, concentrating flavour without sacrificing freshness. That structural chassis is then fleshed out by Barossa Valley and/or Eden Valley Shiraz (~23–45%), sourced from warm, alluvial valley floor sites that contribute mid-palate density and dark-fruited amplitude. In the cellar, wild fermentation in open-top vessels initiates complexity before the wine moves to ~20–21 months in French oak, approximately 41% new barriques — a higher new-oak proportion than The Signature, calibrated to absorb the Coonawarra Cabernet's firm tannin architecture. What sets The Caley apart categorically is that every barrel is sourced through or crafted at Yalumba's on-site cooperage — the only operational winery cooperage in the Southern Hemisphere — giving the team oak-grain control no Australian peer can replicate. The 2017 vintage, not individually noted in the brand library, falls between two documented stellar years (2016 and 2018), suggesting a wine of genuine structural integrity; expect firm tannin and primary dark fruit now, with a cellaring window extending well into the 2030s.

name: Yalumba  Rare & Fine The Signature Cabernet Sauvignon & Shiraz
short_description: Barossa Cab-Shiraz blended since 1962; ~21 months in house-crafted French oak from the Southern Hemisphere's only winery cooperage.
description: When Samuel Smith's descendants decided in 1962 to formalise the Cabernet Sauvignon–Shiraz blend as a house signature, they were codifying a distinctly Australian idiom decades before the category had a name. The Signature has run continuously ever since, making it one of the longest-unbroken premium red programs on the continent. Barossa Valley floor fruit — grown on warm, low-elevation alluvial soils — delivers the mid-palate weight and dark-fruited density that defines the appellation, while the Cabernet component (typically 52–57% of the blend, though no fixed formula is applied) provides the structural counterpoint. Fermentation begins spontaneously in open-top vessels, some of which date to the nineteenth century, before the wine moves to barrel for approximately 21 months in French oak barriques and hogsheads, 25–30% new. Those barrels are not sourced externally: they are hand-crafted on-site at the Yalumba Cooperage, the only fully operational winery-owned cooperage in the Southern Hemisphere, giving the house a degree of oak-character control that no Australian peer can replicate. The 2021 vintage in the Barossa delivered warm, even ripening with sufficient diurnal range to preserve aromatic lift; the wine is approachable now but carries the tannin architecture for a decade of further development.

name: Viu Manent  Vibo Vinedo Centenario
short_description: Colchagua Cabernet-Malbec blend with dark fruit, cedar, and spice—food-friendly Chilean red.
description: Viu Manent's Vibo Vinedo Centenario is a Bordeaux-style red blend from Chile's Colchagua Valley, combining 65% Cabernet Sauvignon with 35% Malbec. The wine displays a medium-full body with balanced tannin structure, offering dark plum and blackcurrant on the nose, supported by cedar and subtle spice. Secondary cocoa and oak notes add complexity, while tertiary leather and tobacco character emerge with air. A versatile, food-friendly expression suited to grilled meats, braised dishes, and aged cheeses.

name: Viu Manent  Vibo Punta del Viento
short_description: Garnacha-led GSM blend from Colchagua Valley with ripe red fruit, spice, and mineral grip—food-friendly and elegant.
description: Viu Manent's Vibo Punta del Viento is a Rhône-inspired blend anchored by Garnacha (71%), with supporting Monastrell and Syrah. Sourced from Chile's Colchagua Valley, this medium-full-bodied red delivers ripe red and dark plum fruit on the primary palate, layered with white pepper and garrigue spice from the Syrah component. The mineral backbone and earthy undertones reflect the vineyard's terroir, while moderate tannins and balanced acidity make this wine approachable across a range of Mediterranean and grilled dishes.

Medium-full body and balanced tannin make this wine versatile at the table, equally at home with grilled meats, braised beef, or herb-forward pasta dishes.

Tertiary tobacco and leather complexity emerges with time, grounding the wine in classic Bordeaux tradition. Medium-full body and balanced tannin structure make this a versatile food companion—equally at home with grilled red meat, braised beef, or aged hard cheese.

name: John Duval  Entity Shiraz

Oak and vanilla from winemaking add richness and complexity, while smoke and chocolate undertones emerge on the mid-palate. Medium acidity and medium-high tannins provide structure without heaviness, making this a versatile choice for both casual enjoyment and fine dining occasions.

Yering Station  Yarra Valley Pinot Noir
short_description: Victoria's oldest vineyard site (est. 1838), wild-yeast Pinot Noir from volcanic loam slopes in Yarra Glen — cool-climate structure over extraction.
description: Yering Station's origins trace to 1838, making it Victoria's — and Australia's — oldest vineyard site, yet every vine standing today dates from a 1988 replanting after decades of abandonment. That gap between heritage and vine age is the estate's defining tension, and it sharpens the winemaking focus entirely onto site expression rather than old-vine mythology. The Yarra Valley's volcanic loam slopes in Yarra Glen provide the primary canvas for this Pinot Noir: free-draining soils over a clay substratum at elevations influenced by cool maritime air, conditions that consistently favour phenolic ripeness ahead of sugar accumulation and preserve the valley's hallmark natural acidity. Wild yeast fermentation drives primary fermentation, with hand-plunging used throughout to extract tannin gently rather than aggressively — a deliberate choice to build structure without weight. French oak is deployed at restrained new-oak percentages (within the estate's documented 16–29% range depending on tier), amplifying texture without obscuring the site's mineral signature. What distinguishes this expression within the Yering portfolio is the annual blind allocation tasting: every parcel is assessed without identity before tier assignment, so the wine in this bottle earned its designation on the vintage's own terms. The Yarra Valley's cool-climate character delivers a taut, site-driven Pinot best approached in its first three to five years for primary fruit clarity, though the structural backbone supports further development.

name: Yering Station  Yarra Valley Reserve Pinot Noir
short_description: Victoria's oldest vineyard site (1838), wild-yeast Pinot Noir blind-allocated to Reserve tier each vintage on merit — Yarra Valley volcanic loam, restrained French oak.
description: Yering Station's annual blind allocation tasting is the structural discipline behind every Reserve designation: all parcels are randomised and assessed without identity before tier assignment, meaning this wine earned its Reserve status on the 2022 vintage's own terms rather than by block formula. The estate's Pinot Noir sites sit on volcanic loam slopes across Yarra Glen and Coldstream — a soil type that retains just enough moisture to sustain vine stress while the cool maritime climate slows ripening, building phenolic complexity well ahead of sugar accumulation. Wild yeast fermentation drives primary fermentation, with hand-plunging replacing mechanical pump-overs for gentler tannin extraction. Aging proceeds in French oak barrels and puncheons with a balance of new and seasoned wood, keeping oak influence subordinate to site character. The 2022 season was moderate in the Yarra Valley — notably, the winemaking team demoted what would have been a Reserve Cabernet Sauvignon to Single Vineyard tier that year, citing insufficient weight, which underscores how strictly the allocation discipline operates. The Reserve Pinot Noir is sealed under screwcap, consistent with the estate's 20-year closure policy. Drink 2025–2030.

name: Beaulieu Vineyard  Georges De Latour Private Reserve Cabernet Sauvignon
short_description: BV Ranch No. 1 & 2, Rutherford Bench — 60-day fermentation across three vessel types, 750 new French oak barrels, Napa's original cult Cabernet since 1936.
description: When André Tchelistcheff arrived at Beaulieu Vineyard in 1938, he simultaneously introduced cold fermentation, malolactic fermentation, and small French oak barrel aging to California — techniques now so universal they are rarely credited to a single address. The Georges de Latour Private Reserve is where all three were first deployed together. BV Ranch No. 1 (127 acres, purchased 1903–1904) and Ranch No. 2 (140 acres) sit on the western Rutherford Bench at the Mayacamas Mountain foothills, where alluvial fan deposits of gravel, sand, and loam drain aggressively, forcing deep root penetration and producing small, intensely concentrated berries. Morning fog off San Pablo Bay extends the growing season, preserving the acidity that anchors the wine's aging architecture. Winemaking is deliberately fractured across three fermentation vessels — 2,000-gallon stainless steel, 1,600-gallon French oak tanks, and 60-gallon new French oak barrels — each contributing a distinct textural layer. Fermentation runs up to 60 days per lot with gentle five-minute hourly pump-overs; malolactic fermentation is completed in barrel. The wine then ages in 750 new French oak barrels for 18–21 months. What no peer replicates is the blend architecture: clonal selections 4, 6, 7, 8, and 169 from the two ranch blocks, with a small Petit Verdot addition for inky depth and violet lift. The 2015 vintage delivered structured, dark-fruited Cabernet from a warm, drought-stressed year that concentrated sugars and amplified the Rutherford Bench's characteristic cocoa-powder tannin texture.

name: Chateau Musar  Red 2017
short_description: Bekaa Valley icon: full-bodied Cabernet-led blend with blackcurrant depth, cedar structure, and age-worthy complexity.
description: Château Musar's 2017 Red represents the Bekaa Valley's signature style—a structured, age-worthy blend anchored by Cabernet Sauvignon with supporting Cinsault and Carignan. The wine opens with dominant blackcurrant and dark plum fruit, layered with cedary oak and warming spice on the mid-palate. High tannins and medium-high acidity provide the backbone for extended cellaring, while tertiary notes of tobacco and leather emerge to reward patience. A wine of serious intent, built for the table and for time.

name: Mount Langi Ghiran  Talus Shiraz
short_description: Grampians Shiraz with dark fruit, pepper spice, and smoky earth—a food-friendly, medium-full red.
description: Mount Langi Ghiran's Talus Shiraz is a single-varietal expression from Australia's Grampians region, showcasing the classic dark-fruit intensity and peppery spice characteristic of cool-climate Shiraz. The wine displays a medium-full body with balanced tannins and a mineral-driven palate, offering layers of blackberry, plum, and licorice with subtle smoke and earth undertones. Structured for food pairing, it bridges casual dining and more formal table settings.

name: Leeuwin Estate  Siblings Shiraz
short_description: Full-bodied Margaret River Shiraz with dark plum, spice, and smoky complexity—a classic Australian red for grilled meats.
description: Leeuwin Estate's Siblings Shiraz is a full-bodied red wine from Margaret River, Australia's premier Shiraz region. The wine opens with dominant dark plum and blackberry fruit, layered with black pepper and licorice notes that define the primary character. Secondary winemaking influence introduces smoke and spice complexity, while tertiary development reveals leather and earthy undertones. Medium-high tannins provide structure and aging potential, making this wine an excellent match for grilled red meats, lamb, and slow-braised beef dishes.

name: Penfolds  Bin 150 Marananga Shiraz
short_description: Marananga-sourced Barossa Shiraz from phylloxera-free ancient soils; 2019 drought compressed the fruit into a dark, structured 'sleeper vintage' Peter Gago likens to 1989.
description: Chief Winemaker Peter Gago publicly designated 2019 a 'sleeper vintage' — a drought year of punishing heat and drastically reduced yields that compressed Barossa Shiraz into dense, inward fruit rather than the opulent generosity of cooler seasons, drawing a direct parallel to the 1989 and 1999 vintages. Bin 150 draws exclusively from Marananga, a sub-district of the Barossa Valley sitting on ancient, deep alluvial and sandy loam soils that have never been touched by phylloxera — old-vine root systems reach unimpeded into subsoil moisture, a structural advantage that proved critical in 2019's water-stressed growing season. The wine is matured in American oak hogsheads, the same vessel format that defines Penfolds' red-wine house style since Max Schubert's era, delivering the characteristic mid-palate richness and coconut-spice integration the house is known for, without the weight of 100% new wood. Where Grange deliberately dissolves terroir into a multi-district blend, Bin 150 inverts that philosophy entirely — it is a single-site Marananga declaration, an anomaly within the Penfolds portfolio that foregrounds place over winemaker assemblage. The 2019 scored 97 pts Decanter and 98 pts Wine Spectator; its compressed dark-fruit architecture demands patience, with a drinking window opening from 2033 through 2070.

name: Hardys  Tintara Reserve Shiraz
short_description: Full-bodied McLaren Vale Shiraz with ripe blackberry, pepper spice, and structured tannins—a classic Australian red.
description: Hardys Tintara Reserve Shiraz is a full-bodied red wine from McLaren Vale, Australia's premier Syrah region. The wine opens with dominant blackberry and dark plum fruit, layered with assertive black pepper and warming spice on the palate. Secondary notes of licorice and smoke add complexity, while tertiary leather and earth characters suggest structure and aging potential. The medium-high tannins provide grip and food-friendliness, making this wine an excellent match for grilled red meats, lamb, and smoky barbecue preparations.

name: Wynns  Coonawarra  Estate Cabernet Shiraz Merlot
